The right column holds a large illustration, photo collage, or simple card stack highlighting 2–3 essential facts (e.g., year founded, group sizes) with plenty of whitespace around them; this block is vertically centered relative to the numbered list so the text and visual feel in balance. On smaller screens, the panel collapses to a single column with the left text content first, followed by the right visual content stacked below, each still surrounded by generous padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the main scroll flow, anchoring the top of the content area associated with the first tab; may be linked to by the tab navigation via in-page anchors.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application start form appears as its own rounded card section further down the page, ideally positioned either directly beneath the overview panel or at the point corresponding to the ‘Application’ tab anchor. It spans the width of the content container with 24–32px padding, maintaining at least 48px of vertical separation from neighboring panels. Within the card, the top-left holds a clear heading such as “Start Your Application” and a short explanatory subtext line, both left-aligned. Below, form fields are arranged in a loose, vertical flow with 16–24px spacing between field groups: brief copy confirming the selected nursery, child details, and perhaps a dropdown for preferred start date. On wide screens, this form can lightly adopt the two-column pattern: the left 60% area hosts the main form fields, while the right 40% contains a soft, pale information sidebar with 1–3 bullet points reminding parents about required documents or linking to the full application guide. Buttons at the bottom (e.g., “Continue” and a secondary link) are aligned to the left, with generous padding and rounded shapes. The overall density keeps each form group visually separated, avoiding a bureaucratic look and preserving the friendly, municipal nursery tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow; may be targeted by a direct jump from a primary CTA in the summary header or tabs, but otherwise behaves like a standard stacked section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The opening hours panel is placed below the application form or in the sequence aligned with its corresponding tab, separated by 48–64px of vertical whitespace. It uses a rounded card spanning the container width with consistent 24–32px padding. Inside, a left-aligned heading such as “Opening Hours” anchors the panel at the top-left, followed by a short, friendly sentence explaining typical daily patterns. The main content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left 55–60% column lists the weekly schedule (e.g., Monday–Friday with time ranges) in a vertical list with 8–12px spacing between days; each day may have a subtle icon or mini badge. The right 40–45% column can hold a compact illustrated clock, a simple timeline graphic, or a small highlight card summarizing key notes like early drop-off or extended care. Line height and spacing are generous so the schedule feels easy to scan and not like a dense timetable. On narrow screens, the schedule list appears first, with the visual or highlight card stacking below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section tied to an in-page anchor used by the ‘Opening Hours’ tab; scrolls normally with surrounding content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The meals and nutrition panel appears as the next rounded section in the vertical stack, again separated from adjacent panels by 48–64px. It spans the container width and follows the soft, watercolor-inspired aesthetic with perhaps a subtle background tint. At the top-left, a heading like “Meals & Nutrition” is followed by a short, bold intro line reassuring parents about healthy, balanced meals. Inside, the layout uses a familiar two-column pattern: the left 60% column contains paragraphs and bullet points describing meal plans, dietary accommodations, and example menus; lists are spaced with 8–16px gaps to keep them light. The right 40% column may contain a stylized weekly meal snapshot (using large, friendly type instead of tight table grids) or a simple illustration of a plate or food icons to echo the child-friendly tone. Visual elements are given whitespace around them, not pressed up against text, so the panel remains breezy and approachable. On smaller screens, text content appears first with the visual stacking underneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scroll flow, reachable via the related tab; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities panel is positioned further down the page, keeping the same 48–64px vertical gap from the previous section. It occupies a full-width rounded rectangle with 24–32px internal padding. At the top-left, a heading like “Facilities & Environment” introduces the section, accompanied by a short, slightly bolder line highlighting key assets (e.g., “Spacious garden, sensory playrooms, and safe sleep areas”). Below, the content may employ a flexible grid inside the left 60% column: 3–6 features are listed as individual rows or cards, each starting with a small circular or icon badge, followed by a concise description. Items are stacked vertically with generous 16–24px gaps, reminiscent of the numbered bullets in the mockup but using icons instead of numbers if more appropriate. The right 40% column can showcase a collage of facility photos, a single wide photo, or a schematic-style illustration of the building and playground. The visual is centered vertically beside the middle of the list to balance the block. As with other sections, on mobile the descriptive list comes first and the visual element stacks beneath it, still within the same rounded panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static stacked section; its heading serves as the anchor target for the ‘Facilities’ tab, but it otherwise scrolls naturally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visits panel appears near the lower part of the page, maintaining the 48–64px separation from the facilities panel. It uses a rounded card spanning the container width with 24–32px padding. A heading like “Visits & Open Days” sits at the top-left, followed by a short inviting sentence. Within the panel, the layout slightly inverts emphasis: the right 40–45% column may contain a small calendar widget or card-style schedule for available visit slots, while the left 55–60% column provides explanatory text about how visits work, what parents can expect, and perhaps a numbered mini-list outlining the visit steps (1. Choose a slot, 2. Confirm contact details, 3. Attend). Each step uses a colored circular number badge aligned in a slender sub-column on the left, matching the visual pattern from the mockup’s numbered bullets, with the explanation text aligned to the right. A primary or secondary button for “Book a Visit” sits near the bottom-left of the panel with generous surrounding whitespace, and alignment to the left maintains consistency with other sections. On smaller screens, text and steps appear first, with any calendar or schedule block stacked below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that may be linked from a visit-related tab or CTA; scrolls with the rest of the content without sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner appears as a slim but noticeable full-width strip within the main content container, positioned directly below the nursery summary header and above the tab navigation. It spans the same width as the content container but is much shallower than the main cards, with about 12–16px of vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding. The background uses a warm but alert color (e.g., soft red or amber) paired with rounded corners to keep it consistent with the overall friendly aesthetic rather than harsh. Text is left-aligned and concise, with an icon or small illustration on the far left occupying a narrow column, followed by the message and any inline link or retry action. There is a 16–24px gap below the banner before the tabs, so it doesn’t feel visually glued to them. When there is no error, this banner disappears entirely and the spacing between the summary header and tabs closes slightly, maintaining a clean flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the scroll flow when an error state occurs; does not stick or float and collapses completely when not needed.

name: Favorites
title: Favorites
description: Manage nurseries saved to your favorites: view key information, open nursery details, remove favorites, and start applications or visit bookings.

core_features: Load and display the current user's favorite nurseries using getFavoriteNurseries, including name, district, address, distance, rating, fee, opening time, and care types.
Allow navigation from any favorite nursery to its detail page (nursery_detail.html) via nurseryId URL parameter.
Remove nurseries from the favorites list by calling toggleNurseryFavorite with mode set to remove, updating the list and total count in the UI.
Start a new nursery place application from one or more selected favorite nurseries using startApplicationWithNurseries, then navigate to application.html with the created applicationId.
Provide a direct action on each favorite nursery to book a visit, navigating to visit_booking.html with the nurseryId URL parameter.
Support client-side sorting and filtering of favorite nurseries (e.g., by distance, rating, fee, district, care type) controlled by URL parameters.
Handle empty favorites state with informative messaging and without hardcoded sample data or nurseries.
user_workflows: View and manage favorite nurseries: 1) User navigates to favorites.html (via header link or direct link). 2) The page controller reads URL parameters such as sort, sortDir, filterDistrict, careFilter, view, highlightNurseryId, and startApplicationNurseryId. 3) The page calls getFavoriteNurseries with no parameters. 4) Once data is returned, the controller stores the list in page state, applies sorting and filtering based on URL parameters, and renders the favorites list. 5) If highlightNurseryId is present, the corresponding nursery card is visually marked in the list (purely as state, no extra data calls).

Open nursery details from favorites: 1) User clicks on a nursery card area or its title in the favorites list. 2) The favorites-page-controller uses the clicked item's nurseryId to construct the URL nursery_detail.html?nurseryId=<nurseryId>. 3) Browser navigates to the nursery detail page, which is responsible for its own data loading.

Remove a nursery from favorites: 1) User clicks the remove-from-favorites control (e.g., a button) on a nursery card. 2) The favorite-toggle-button component calls toggleNurseryFavorite with nurseryId from the card and mode set to remove. 3) On success, the component notifies the page controller with the updated isFavorite and totalFavorites values. 4) The controller removes the nursery from the in-memory favorites list and re-renders the favorites-list component according to the current sort and filter URL parameters. 5) If the list becomes empty, the favorites-empty-state component is displayed instead. 6) On failure, an error message is shown via the notification-banner, and the list is not modified.

Start an application for a single favorite nursery: 1) User clicks the "Apply for a place" action on a specific favorite nursery card. 2) The nursery-actions-panel notifies the page controller, passing the nurseryId. 3) The controller opens the application-start-modal with selectedNurseryIds set to an array containing that nurseryId. 4) In the modal, the user selects or enters desiredStartDate, careType, and scheduleType. 5) On submit, application-start-modal calls startApplicationWithNurseries with parameters: nurseryIds set to the current selectedNurseryIds array, desiredStartDate and careType and scheduleType from the form, source set to a valid enum value (for example, nursery_search), and districtFilter derived from the selected favorites if applicable or left unset. 6) On success, the modal closes and the controller navigates to application.html?applicationId=<returned Application.id>. 7) On failure, the modal remains open and displays an error message using the notification-banner or inline messaging.

Start an application for multiple selected favorite nurseries: 1) User activates bulk selection mode via the favorites-toolbar. 2) The favorites-list renders selection controls (e.g., checkboxes) for each favorite-nursery-card, and the user selects one or more nurseries. 3) The selectedNurseryIds are stored in page state and reflected in the bulk-application-selection-bar. 4) User clicks the "Apply to selected" action in the bulk-application-selection-bar. 5) The application-start-modal opens with selectedNurseryIds set to all selected favorites. 6) User fills in desiredStartDate, careType, and scheduleType, then submits. 7) application-start-modal calls startApplicationWithNurseries with nurseryIds equal to the selectedNurseryIds array, other parameters from the modal form, and a suitable source value. 8) On success, the controller clears the current selection and navigates to application.html?applicationId=<returned Application.id>. 9) On failure, an error is displayed; selection is preserved so the user can retry or adjust inputs.

Book a visit from favorites: 1) User clicks the "Book a visit" action for a favorite nursery in the nursery-actions-panel. 2) The component passes the nurseryId to the page controller. 3) The controller constructs the URL visit_booking.html?nurseryId=<nurseryId>. 4) Browser navigates to the visit booking page, which handles visit slot loading and booking logic.

Adjust sorting, filtering, and view mode: 1) User changes sort options (e.g., selects a different sort field or direction) in the favorites-toolbar. 2) favorites-toolbar notifies the controller with new sort and direction values. 3) The controller updates the URL query parameters (e.g., ?sort=<value>&sortDir=<value>) using the History API without reloading the page. 4) The controller re-applies sorting to the in-memory favorites list and re-renders favorites-list. 5) Similarly, when the user changes filters (district, care type, rating threshold, fee range) or view mode, the toolbar updates the corresponding URL parameters; the controller re-filters the list client-side and updates the rendering accordingly.

Handle empty favorites or direct links: 1) If getFavoriteNurseries returns an empty array, the controller renders favorites-empty-state instead of favorites-list. 2) If the page is accessed via a direct link from another page (for example, after adding a favorite) with highlightNurseryId or startApplicationNurseryId in the URL, the controller uses highlightNurseryId to visually emphasize a specific card, and optionally opens application-start-modal automatically for startApplicationNurseryId if the nursery exists in the favorites list. 3) This behavior is entirely controlled by URL parameters and does not rely on localStorage.
interactions: On page load (favorites-page-controller.onPageLoad): read query parameters from the URL, call getFavoriteNurseries, store the response in state, apply initial sort/filter logic, and render either favorites-list or favorites-empty-state.
Click on nursery card or title (favorite-nursery-card.onClickOpenDetails): navigate to nursery_detail.html?nurseryId=<nurseryId> using the nurseryId from the clicked card.

Click on remove favorite button (favorite-toggle-button.onClick): call toggleNurseryFavorite with nurseryId from the card and mode='remove'; on success, update the in-memory favorites array and re-render; on failure, show an error via notification-banner.

Toggle favorite state (if supported) from this page (favorite-toggle-button.onClick): if the design allows re-adding from the same control, call toggleNurseryFavorite with mode='add' when the nursery is not currently marked as favorite in state; update the list and totalFavorites based on the response.

Click on "Apply for a place" on a card (nursery-actions-panel.onClickApply): open application-start-modal with selectedNurseryIds set to the current nurseryId; user then interacts with the modal form and submits to trigger startApplicationWithNurseries.
Click on "Book a visit" on a card (nursery-actions-panel.onClickBookVisit): navigate to visit_booking.html?nurseryId=<nurseryId>.
Change sort option (favorites-toolbar.onSortChange): update sort and sortDir URL parameters via History API; re-sort the in-memory favorites list; re-render favorites-list.

Change filters (favorites-toolbar.onFilterChange): update filter-related URL parameters (filterDistrict, careFilter, ratingMin, feeMax, etc. as defined); re-filter the in-memory favorites list; re-render favorites-list without additional API calls.
Toggle view mode (favorites-toolbar.onViewChange): update view=<mode> URL parameter; instruct favorites-list to render either full or compact layout, without changing data.

Select a nursery for bulk application (favorite-nursery-card.onSelectForBulkApply): toggle the nursery’s presence in selectedNurseryIds in page state; update bulk-application-selection-bar visibility and contents.

Start bulk application (bulk-application-selection-bar.onClickApplyToSelected): open application-start-modal pre-populated with all selectedNurseryIds; on submit, call startApplicationWithNurseries and navigate to application.html on success.

Clear bulk selection (bulk-application-selection-bar.onClickClearSelection): clear selectedNurseryIds from page state, update URL if a dedicated parameter for selection mode is used, and re-render favorites-list without selection markers.

Submit application-start-modal (application-start-modal.onSubmit): validate required fields, call startApplicationWithNurseries, handle success by navigating to application.html?applicationId=<id> and closing the modal, or handle errors by showing messages and keeping the modal open.

Close/cancel application-start-modal (application-start-modal.onCancel or onClose): close the modal without calling startApplicationWithNurseries; leave URL parameters unchanged unless a specific startApplicationNurseryId parameter was used, in which case the controller may remove it via History API.

Show API feedback (notification-banner.onShow / onDismiss): when toggleNurseryFavorite or startApplicationWithNurseries returns messages or errors, display them in a dismissible notification; dismissing the banner does not trigger additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Favorites page maintains its state using in-memory data structures derived from API responses and URL query parameters. No localStorage or other persistent browser storage is used. On initial load, the favorites-page-controller parses the current window.location.search to read parameters such as: sort (current sort field, e.g., distance, rating, fee, addedAt), sortDir (sort direction, e.g., asc or desc), filterDistrict (selected district filter, matching the Nursery.district enum values), careFilter (indicating whether to show only nurseries with full day care, half day care, or both, based on hasFullDayCare and hasHalfDayCare fields), ratingMin and feeMax (optional numeric thresholds for client-side filtering using ratingAverage and baseMonthlyFee), view (view mode such as list or compact, controlling how favorites-list renders), highlightNurseryId (if present, used to highlight a specific nursery card after loading), startApplicationNurseryId (if present, used to automatically open application-start-modal for a specific favorite nursery after data load, assuming that nursery exists in the favorites list). After reading URL parameters, the controller calls getFavoriteNurseries once to retrieve the entire favorites dataset. All subsequent sort and filter operations are applied client-side on this dataset without additional API calls. When the user changes sort, filter, or view options via favorites-toolbar, the controller updates the corresponding URL parameters using the History API (pushState or replaceState) and then re-applies the logic to the in-memory list. When favorites are removed via toggleNurseryFavorite, the controller updates the in-memory list and may optionally update a URL parameter that reflects total favorites count if such a parameter is defined, though this is not required. Starting an application does not alter the Favorites page URL except that the controller may clear startApplicationNurseryId to avoid reopening the modal on back-navigation. Navigation to nursery_detail.html, application.html, and visit_booking.html always uses URL parameters defined in the navigation information: nursery_detail.html?nurseryId=<id>, application.html?applicationId=<id>, visit_booking.html?nurseryId=<id>. On browser back/forward navigation, the controller listens for URL changes (popstate), re-parses the query parameters, and re-applies sorting, filtering, highlighting, and possible automatic modal opening based on the new parameters, without recalling getFavoriteNurseries unless a full reload occurs.

components.functionality: Central controller for the Favorites page that initializes data, coordinates API calls, reads and updates URL parameters, manages in-memory state (favorites list, selection, sorting, filtering, highlighting), and orchestrates communication between child components.
Encapsulates the call to getFavoriteNurseries, handles loading and error states, and passes the resulting array of favorites to the page controller.

Provides controls for sorting (field and direction), filtering (by district, care type, ratings, fees), toggling view mode, and enabling bulk selection mode for applications. Updates URL parameters via the page controller when the user changes any control.

Displays the list of favorite nurseries according to the current sort, filter, view mode, and selection state. Renders one favorite-nursery-card per favorite returned by getFavoriteNurseries. Handles empty list by delegating to favorites-empty-state.

Represents a single favorite nursery entry with its key information (name, district, address, distanceFromHomeKm, ratingAverage, ratingCount, baseMonthlyFee with currency, opensByTimeWeekday, hasFullDayCare, hasHalfDayCare) and exposes actions to open details, start an application, book a visit, select for bulk application, and remove from favorites.
Button or control that toggles the favorite state of a nursery from within the favorites list, primarily used to remove a nursery from favorites by calling toggleNurseryFavorite.

Grouping of actions for each favorite nursery card, including starting an application and booking a visit. Delegates to the page controller to open the application-start-modal or navigate to visit_booking.html.

Appears when at least one favorite nursery is selected for bulk application. Summarizes the number of selected nurseries and provides actions to start an application for all selected nurseries or to clear the selection.

Modal form used to collect the parameters needed to call startApplicationWithNurseries: selectedNurseryIds (provided by the controller), desiredStartDate, careType, and scheduleType. On submit, it calls startApplicationWithNurseries and, on success, triggers navigation to application.html with the returned applicationId.
Displays an informative message and guidance when getFavoriteNurseries returns no favorites. It does not rely on or display any static nursery data and does not call APIs.

Shows transient or persistent messages for the user, such as success or error messages from toggleNurseryFavorite and startApplicationWithNurseries. It is controlled by the page controller and does not directly call APIs.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom stacking: a hero-like favorites toolbar at the top inside a large rounded rectangle, followed by the main favorites content in a two-column layout. The left column (about 60%) carries the functional content (favorites list or empty state text), while the right column (about 40%) is reserved for a large, friendly illustration and light guidance, echoing the original ‘Üdvözlet’ section. Global/system elements (notification banner, bulk selection bar, modal) overlay or sit above/below this vertical flow without breaking it.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s top-to-bottom stacking: a hero-like favorites toolbar at the top inside a large rounded rectangle, followed by the main favorites content in a two-column layout. The left column (about 60%) carries the functional content (favorites list or empty state text), while the right column (about 40%) is reserved for a large, friendly illustration and light guidance, echoing the original ‘Üdvözlet’ section. Global/system elements (notification banner, bulk selection bar, modal) overlay or sit above/below this vertical flow without breaking it.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered, fixed-width content container (roughly 1120–1200px) sits on a fluid, softly colored background. The hero-like favorites toolbar spans the full width of this content container and uses generous inset padding (24–32px). Below it, the main section is split into two columns: about 55–60% width for the favorites list on the left and 40–45% for the decorative/illustrative side and helper text on the right, maintaining breathing room. Section gaps are 48–64px vertically, and cards within the list are separated by 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is deliberately low. Typography is large and well-spaced, with comfortable line height and clear separation (16–24px) between headings, descriptive text, and controls. Cards have ample internal padding (24–28px), and the right column often shows illustration and short, friendly helpers instead of dense controls, preserving the warm, editorial feel. Actions are grouped rather than scattered, reducing cognitive load.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow repeats the mockup’s Z-pattern: the eye lands on the large page title and summary at the top-left inside the favorites toolbar, then moves across to the top-right where key filters and a primary action button live. From there, the gaze drops diagonally down to the first favorite card in the left column, then scans horizontally within each card from the nursery name and info on the left to the favorite toggle and action buttons on the right. The decorative right column and, when active, the sticky bulk selection bar at the bottom-right provide secondary focal points that balance the layout and anchor the page visually.

layout.overall_description: The Favorites page sits within a softly colored, child-friendly background, with the main content held in a centered, rounded container that feels like a warm card on the page. At the top, a broad, rounded hero-style panel announces the Favorites section with a big, friendly heading, a short descriptive sentence, and key filters and a primary action button arranged toward the right, mirroring the hero slider structure. Below this, the page opens into a two-column layout: on the left, a vertical stack of roomy nursery cards makes up the favorites list; on the right, a generous column holds an illustration and brief helper text that guides parents in using favorites and applications. Each nursery card has a clear left-to-right rhythm, with name and details on the left and a heart-like favorite toggle and action buttons grouped on the right, echoing the mockup’s balance between text and decorative elements. A slim notification banner can appear just above the hero panel, and when multiple items are selected, a pill-like bulk selection bar gently pins itself to the bottom of the content area, aligning with the right edge and reinforcing the Z-shaped visual path. A modal, when opened to start an application, floats centrally over a dimmed background, preserving the rounded, illustrated design language and keeping the playful, municipal nursery character consistent across the experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-page-controller acts as the main page frame. It centers a fixed-width content column (approximately 1120–1200px wide) within a fluid, full-width background that uses a soft, warm color or watercolor wash. There is generous top padding (48–64px) beneath the global site header and equal bottom padding to visually separate this page from the viewport edges. All visible components—notification-banner, favorites-toolbar, main two-column content, and overlays—are visually and structurally contained within this centered frame. The container’s corners are subtly rounded, echoing the hero-like rounded rectangle from the mockup, and content inside aligns to an invisible 12-column grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page wrapper that defines the centered content area and governs the grid and vertical spacing for all inner components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-data-loader overlays or replaces the main content area (favorites-toolbar and favorites-list/right-column region) while data is being fetched. It respects the rounded shape and padding of the underlying panels: within the hero-like toolbar area, it may show skeleton blocks where the heading and filters would be; within the left column, it shows a vertical stack of card-shaped skeletons occupying the same width as the favorite-nursery-card instances. The loader covers roughly the same vertical height that a typical non-empty favorites list would occupy, leaving the global page padding intact, and sits centered within the controller’s content width, keeping edge distances consistent with other content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the main content region (within the centered container). When active, it blocks interaction with underlying list content but does not obscure global navigation or hypothetical floating controls like scroll-to-top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-toolbar is positioned near the top of the page, immediately below any notification-banner and within the centered content container. It appears as a large, rounded rectangle spanning the full content width (minus standard side padding of 24–32px from the page controller), visually echoing the hero slider from the mockup. Its height is substantial but not full-screen—roughly 220–260px depending on content. Inside, the left two-thirds host a bold, oversized page title (e.g., “Kedvencek”), a short explanatory paragraph, and possibly a small count of saved nurseries stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing. The right third holds compact controls aligned to the top-right: a filter dropdown, a search field, and potentially a primary button like “Új jelentkezés indítása,” aligned so the primary button is closest to the edge. Internal padding of 24–32px ensures ample whitespace around the text and controls. The overall feel is that of a welcoming, playful control hub that mirrors the hero’s left-text/right-interaction balance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-of-content section; scrolls away with the page and does not stick, maintaining a traditional top-to-bottom flow consistent with the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-list sits directly below the favorites-toolbar, separated by a vertical gap of about 48–56px to keep sections distinct. It occupies the left column of a two-column layout within the main content area. This left column takes up approximately 55–60% of the content width and aligns with the left edge of the toolbar’s text. The list is a vertical stack of favorite-nursery-card components, each spanning the full width of this column. Cards are separated by 16–24px of vertical space, creating a comfortable, low-density rhythm. The top of the list aligns horizontally with the top of the right-side illustration/helper area, reinforcing the two-column structure. If the list is short, it sits naturally in the upper part of the viewport with generous whitespace below; if long, it extends downward with the right column content ending earlier, allowing the left side to scroll with more items while the right side scrolls away in sync.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column content within the main section, scrollable with the page; it dynamically yields its space to the favorites-empty-state when no favorites exist.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each favorite-nursery-card is a horizontally oriented, rounded rectangle that fills the full width of the favorites-list left column, with 24–28px of internal padding on all sides. The card’s internal layout follows a left-to-right structure: the left 70–75% of the card is dedicated to nursery information—nursery name as a prominent heading at the top, followed by key details such as address, age range, and availability, stacked with 8–16px spacing. The right 25–30% area is reserved for actions and status: the favorite-toggle-button sits near the top-right corner, slightly inset from the edges, while the nursery-actions-panel is anchored along the bottom-right, forming a compact group of buttons. This arrangement creates a subtle Z within each card: from the top-left nursery name, across to the favorite toggle, then down to the action buttons. Cards are visually anchored to the left column but have enough right padding to avoid feeling cramped against the column edge, maintaining the soft, open feeling of the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static list item within the favorites-list, expanding vertically as needed for content while maintaining consistent width and padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-toggle-button appears inside each favorite-nursery-card, positioned in the top-right region of the card’s content area. It is a small, circular or pill-shaped button, roughly 32–40px in diameter, with an icon (heart or star) centered inside and a warm accent color background that matches the mockup’s yellow badges. It is inset by about 16px from the card’s right edge and 16–20px from the top edge, visually floating above the main text but clearly anchored to the card. Its placement mirrors the numbered circles from the mockup, acting as a compact badge-like control that stands out without overwhelming the card. The button does not extend beyond the card’s rounded boundary, preserving the soft silhouette of the card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the card; its absolute or flex alignment is anchored to the card’s top-right, remaining in place regardless of text length below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nursery-actions-panel is placed at the bottom of each favorite-nursery-card, aligned to the right side of the card’s content area. It consists of a horizontal group of 2–3 buttons (e.g., “Részletek megnyitása,” “Jelentkezés indítása,” “Látogatás foglalása”) with 8–12px spacing between them. The right-most primary action uses a more saturated, playful color to catch the eye, echoing the hero’s right-side controls. The panel sits about 16–20px above the bottom padding of the card and is vertically separated from the descriptive text area above by 16–24px, keeping actions distinct from information. On smaller widths, the button group can wrap to two lines but remains visually grouped and right-aligned, maintaining the card’s left-info/right-actions balance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each card, anchored to the bottom-right portion of the card content, adjusting layout responsively but staying grouped as a unit.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bulk-application-selection-bar appears only when at least one favorite is selected. It is a rounded, pill-like bar that spans most of the content width near the bottom of the viewport, visually echoing the mockup’s floating scroll-to-top element but larger and aligned to the main container. Horizontally, it is centered within the page controller’s content width but may be slightly biased to the right to reinforce the Z-pattern, leaving a bit more whitespace on the left than on the right. It sits 24–32px above the bottom edge of the viewport and 24–32px in from the left and right edges of the centered container. Inside, the left side of the bar displays the count of selected nurseries and a brief description, while the right side holds a prominent “Jelentkezés indítása” bulk action button and possibly a secondary “Kiválasztás törlése” text button. The bar has generous vertical padding (12–16px) and casts a very soft shadow or elevation effect to float above the content, similar in presence to the hero’s arrows but anchored at the bottom.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky/floating at the bottom of the viewport within the centered content area; it overlays but does not obscure the lower parts of cards, and scrolls away only when dismissed or when selection is cleared.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-start-modal appears centered in the viewport, on top of a semi-transparent dimmed overlay that covers the entire page, including the hero-like toolbar and list. The modal itself is a rounded rectangle panel, about 40–50% of the viewport width on desktop, with a maximum width aligned to the main content grid but visually independent of the container edges. It has generous padding (32px) and a vertical layout: a bold, warm heading at the top, a short explanatory paragraph, and a structured form (e.g., child details, preferred start date) with 16–24px spacing between form groups. At the bottom of the modal, aligned to the right, sit a primary “Jelentkezés indítása” button and a secondary “Mégsem” or “Bezárás” action. The modal’s design language mirrors the cards and hero sections—soft edges, large friendly type, and subtle illustration accents or icons near the top-left corner—to preserve the child-friendly aesthetic even in this more functional moment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed in the center of the viewport, overlaying all page content with a dimmed backdrop; scroll is confined to the modal if content extends beyond its height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-empty-state replaces the favorites-list and right-column content when there are no saved nurseries. It is positioned in the same vertical slot below the favorites-toolbar, centered within the content container. The empty state is presented as a large, rounded rectangle card that spans approximately 70–80% of the content width, maintaining 24–32px side margins from the container. Inside, it echoes the mockup’s ‘Üdvözlet’ two-column layout: the left 55–60% contains a big friendly heading (e.g., “Még nincsenek kedvencek”), a bold introductory sentence explaining the benefit of saving nurseries, and 2–3 short, numbered steps arranged vertically with yellow circular badges on the far left and explanatory text to their right, separated by 16–24px. The right 40–45% shows a large, playful illustration of children, hearts, and a house, mirroring the original mockup’s artwork. The entire empty state card sits with generous top and bottom margins (48–64px) so it feels like a central welcome panel rather than a small notice.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section content that occupies the main two-column region when the list is empty, pushing other list elements out of view but remaining within the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner sits at the very top of the favorites-page-controller’s content area, directly beneath the site-wide header and above the favorites-toolbar. It spans the full width of the content container with slightly rounded corners, resembling a slim, colorful strip compared to the larger hero panel below. Its height is modest (around 56–72px), with 16–20px of vertical padding inside. The banner’s text is centered vertically and aligned to the left, with any dismiss or action buttons aligned to the right edge inside the same row. There is a gap of about 16–24px between the bottom of the banner and the top of the favorites-toolbar, allowing each element to breathe. The banner’s color and iconography harmonize with the playful palette—using soft yellows, greens, or reds—but it remains visually secondary to the hero toolbar, acting as a temporary, lightweight message layer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the top of the content container; it pushes the favorites-toolbar downward while visible and scrolls away with the page content.

name: Visit Search
title: Visit Search
description: Search for municipal nurseries available for visits, filter them by your needs, sort the results, and start the visit booking process.

core_features: Load available filter, fee, and sorting options for visit search using getVisitSearchFilterOptions.
Initialize search filters from incoming URL parameters (childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating, sortBy).
Execute nursery search for visits using searchNurseriesForVisit with the current filter and sort values.

Display a list of nursery result cards, each showing key details (name, district, distanceFromHomeKm, ratingAverage, ratingCount, baseMonthlyFee, currency, opensByTimeWeekday, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, earliestAvailableVisitDateTime).
Allow users to adjust filters (child age, max distance, large outdoor playground, maximum monthly fee, minimum rating) and re-run the search.
Allow users to sort results using available sortOptions (e.g., by rating, distance, or earliest available visit/opening time) and re-run the search with the chosen sortBy value.
Provide a "Book a visit" action on each nursery card that navigates to visit_booking.html with the selected nurseryId in the URL.
Provide a way to open the nursery detail page (nursery_detail.html) by clicking the nursery name or an explicit "View details" action, passing nurseryId in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Access Visit Search from header without pre-filters
1. User navigates via the header link to visits.html without query parameters.
2. The page-level state manager parses the URL; no filter parameters are found, so only default internal state is set.
3. The page calls getVisitSearchFilterOptions to obtain available maxDistanceKmOptions, facilityOptions, ratingOptions, feeRangeSuggestions, and sortOptions.
4. The filter component initializes its fields using the options from getVisitSearchFilterOptions (for example, choosing a neutral or first available option for distance, rating, fee, and sort where applicable) without hardcoding values.
5. The page constructs a searchNurseriesForVisit request using the initialized filter state (childAgeYears may be unset/null, and other filters may be non-restrictive according to current UI state) and a sortBy value derived from the sortOptions list.
6. searchNurseriesForVisit returns an array of nurseries; the results list component renders a card for each nursery.
7. The user reviews the results and either refines filters/sorting or chooses a nursery to book a visit or view details.

Workflow: Access Visit Search from index.html with preselected filters
1. From index.html, the user triggers an action (e.g., "Find nurseries to visit") that navigates to visits.html with query parameters such as childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, and minRating.
2. On loading visits.html, the state manager reads these URL parameters and stores them as the initial filter state.
3. The page calls getVisitSearchFilterOptions to load option lists and labels.
4. The filter component initializes its fields using a combination of URL parameters (taking precedence where provided) and option defaults from getVisitSearchFilterOptions.
5. The page calls searchNurseriesForVisit with childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating, and an initial sortBy value (from URL if present, otherwise derived from sortOptions).
6. Results are displayed, reflecting the pre-applied filters so the user immediately sees nurseries matching the initial criteria.
7. The URL remains in sync with the current filters and sort (e.g., via pushState or query string updates) so that reloading or sharing the URL reproduces the same search.

Workflow: Refine filters and sorting on the Visit Search page
1. The user changes a filter value in the filter component (e.g., adjusts childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, toggles hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, changes maxMonthlyFee, or minRating).
2. The filter component updates the in-memory filter state and notifies the state manager that filters have changed.
3. The state manager updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new filter values (e.g., updating childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating) while keeping unrelated parameters (like sortBy) intact.
4. The state manager calls searchNurseriesForVisit again with the updated filter values and the current sortBy.
5. The results list component re-renders with the new array returned from searchNurseriesForVisit.
6. If the user changes the sort option (e.g., selects a different entry from sortOptions), the sort control updates sortBy, writes it to the URL, and triggers a new searchNurseriesForVisit call, leading to re-sorted results.

Workflow: Book a visit from a nursery result
1. The user scans the results list and identifies a suitable nursery card.
2. The user clicks the "Book a visit" button within that nursery card.
3. The nursery card component emits an event with the nurseryId of the selected nursery.
4. The state manager (or a navigation handler) constructs a URL for visit_booking.html that includes the nurseryId as a query parameter (e.g., visit_booking.html?nurseryId={selectedNursery.nurseryId}).
5. The browser navigates to visit_booking.html, where the booking workflow continues (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: View nursery details from a result card
1. The user sees a nursery in the results list and wants more information.
2. The user clicks either the nursery name or a dedicated "View details" action on the card.
3. The nursery card component emits a navigation event with the current nurseryId.
4. A navigation handler composes a URL nursery_detail.html?nurseryId={selectedNursery.nurseryId}.
5. The browser navigates to nursery_detail.html, where full nursery details are displayed (outside the scope of this page).

Workflow: Reload or share the Visit Search URL
1. The user has configured filters and sort on visits.html and the state manager has synchronized these values into the URL query parameters.
2. The user reloads the page or shares the URL with another person.
3. On load, the state manager reads the query parameters (childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating, sortBy) and uses them to initialize the filter and sort state.
4. getVisitSearchFilterOptions is called to obtain valid option lists and labels.
5. The filter and sort components adjust their displayed values to match the URL-based state while ensuring they remain consistent with the available options.
6. searchNurseriesForVisit is called with these restored values, and the same results (subject to backend changes) are displayed, reproducing the shared or reloaded state.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: visits.html is loaded.
- Behavior: Parse URL query parameters for childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating, and sortBy. Call getVisitSearchFilterOptions. Initialize filter and sort UI based on URL parameters and the returned options. Then call searchNurseriesForVisit with the resulting filter and sort state and render the returned nurseries.

Interaction: Update child age filter
- Trigger: User changes the child age input (e.g., via a numeric input or selector) in the filter component.
- Behavior: Update in-memory childAgeYears state. Update the childAgeYears query parameter in the URL. Re-call searchNurseriesForVisit with the new childAgeYears and current values for other filters and sortBy. Update the results list with the new data.

Interaction: Update max distance filter
- Trigger: User selects a max distance option from a control bound to maxDistanceKmOptions.
- Behavior: Update in-memory maxDistanceKm state. Persist maxDistanceKm to the URL. Re-call searchNurseriesForVisit with updated maxDistanceKm and other current filters/sortBy. Re-render results.

Interaction: Toggle large outdoor playground filter
- Trigger: User toggles a checkbox or switch for hasLargeOutdoorPlayground.
- Behavior: Set hasLargeOutdoorPlayground state to reflect the control (e.g., flagged when checked). Update the hasLargeOutdoorPlayground URL parameter (for example using a string representation as required by navigation). Call searchNurseriesForVisit with the updated value and other current filters/sortBy. Update results.

Interaction: Update maximum monthly fee filter
- Trigger: User modifies the maximum monthly fee control (e.g., slider or input) informed by feeRangeSuggestions.
- Behavior: Update maxMonthlyFee in state. Update the maxMonthlyFee query parameter in the URL. Call searchNurseriesForVisit with updated maxMonthlyFee and current values for remaining parameters. Re-render results.

Interaction: Update minimum rating filter
- Trigger: User selects a minimum rating from a control bound to ratingOptions.
- Behavior: Update minRating in state. Reflect minRating in the URL. Call searchNurseriesForVisit with updated minRating and other filters/sortBy. Re-render results.

Interaction: Change sort order
- Trigger: User selects a sort option from a list bound to sortOptions (using each option's value as sortBy).
- Behavior: Update sortBy state. Update sortBy in the URL. Call searchNurseriesForVisit with the existing filter values and new sortBy. Re-render results list in the new order.

Interaction: Click "Book a visit" on a nursery card
- Trigger: User clicks the primary booking button on a nursery card.
- Behavior: Capture the nurseryId from that card's bound data. Navigate to visit_booking.html, appending nurseryId as a query parameter as defined in outgoing navigation rules.

Interaction: Click nursery name or "View details"
- Trigger: User clicks either the clickable nursery name or a specific "View details" action on a nursery card.
- Behavior: Capture the nurseryId from the card's data. Navigate to nursery_detail.html with nurseryId as a query parameter as defined in outgoing navigation rules.

Interaction: Handle empty search results
- Trigger: searchNurseriesForVisit returns an empty array.
- Behavior: The results list component hides the list and shows an empty-state message while still displaying the active filters and sort controls so the user can adjust and retry; no static nursery data is shown.

Interaction: Browser navigation (back/forward) with query string changes
- Trigger: User uses browser back/forward buttons causing URL query parameters to change.
- Behavior: The state manager detects URL changes, re-parses the current query parameters, updates filter and sort UI to match, and calls searchNurseriesForVisit with the new parameters to keep the view consistent with navigation history.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully derived from and synchronized with URL parameters and interface responses.

Initial state on page load:
- The state manager reads the following URL parameters, if present: childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating, sortBy.
- These values are parsed into the internal filter and sort state. Missing parameters are left undefined or set to non-restrictive internal defaults (e.g., no maximum distance or no minimum rating), but these defaults are not hardcoded numeric values and may depend on getVisitSearchFilterOptions outputs.
- The page calls getVisitSearchFilterOptions to obtain:
  - maxDistanceKmOptions: used to populate the distance filter control and to validate/adjust any maxDistanceKm value read from the URL.
  - facilityOptions.largeOutdoorPlaygroundLabel: used as the label for the hasLargeOutdoorPlayground control.
  - ratingOptions: used to populate the minimum rating selector and to validate/adjust minRating.
  - feeRangeSuggestions: used to guide the maximum monthly fee control and to constrain/interpret maxMonthlyFee if needed.
  - sortOptions: used to populate the sorting control and to validate/adjust sortBy. If URL sortBy is absent or not found in sortOptions, the page chooses a default based on the options list (e.g., first available), not by hardcoding.
- After filter and sort state is initialized and validated against these option lists, the page calls searchNurseriesForVisit with:
  - childAgeYears: from URL or internal default.
  - maxDistanceKm: from URL or internal default.
  - hasLargeOutdoorPlayground: derived from URL parameter (e.g., set when URL string equals a designated truthy value) or internal default.
  - maxMonthlyFee: from URL or internal default.
  - minRating: from URL or internal default.
  - sortBy: from URL or chosen from sortOptions.
- The returned array of nurseries is stored in state and bound to the results list component.

State updates during interaction:
- Whenever a filter or sort control changes, the state manager updates the corresponding URL parameter using the new value while preserving other query parameters. This can be done via history.pushState or a full reload; the design is neutral on implementation.
- Immediately after updating the URL, the state manager calls searchNurseriesForVisit with the new full set of parameters so that the data always corresponds to the values visible in the URL.
- The components never rely on localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; the URL query string is the single source of truth for current filter and sort configuration.

URL-driven behavior:
- Reloading visits.html with an existing query string reconstructs the same filter and sort state, leading to a call to searchNurseriesForVisit with those parameters.
- Using back/forward navigation changes the URL; the state manager re-reads the parameters and re-fetches results using searchNurseriesForVisit to keep the UI synchronized.
- Outgoing navigation to visit_booking.html and nursery_detail.html always includes nurseryId in the URL query string, allowing those pages to load the appropriate data without relying on shared client state.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, interface calls, filter/sort state, and data flow between components on visits.html. On load, it reads URL parameters, calls getVisitSearchFilterOptions, initializes filter and sort state, calls searchNurseriesForVisit, and distributes the results to child components. It also updates the URL and re-calls searchNurseriesForVisit whenever child components signal filter or sort changes.

Provides user controls to set search filters for visit search, including child age, maximum distance, large outdoor playground requirement, maximum monthly fee, and minimum rating. It receives available option lists from getVisitSearchFilterOptions via the state manager and displays current values derived from URL parameters. When the user changes any filter, it emits an event with the updated filter state.

Displays the available sort options returned by getVisitSearchFilterOptions.sortOptions and allows the user to select the current sort order (e.g., by rating, distance, or earliest visit/opening time). The currently active sortBy value is synchronized with the URL and with the state manager. Changing the selection triggers a new searchNurseriesForVisit call via the state manager.

Shows a brief summary of the current search context, such as the number of results returned by searchNurseriesForVisit and a textual representation of currently applied filters (child age, max distance, playground requirement, maximum monthly fee, minimum rating, and sort order). This component is purely informational and updates whenever new results or filter/sort changes occur.

Renders a list of nursery cards based on the array returned from searchNurseriesForVisit. It creates one nursery card component per element, passing through all relevant fields (nurseryId, name, district, distanceFromHomeKm, ratingAverage, ratingCount, baseMonthlyFee, currency, opensByTimeWeekday, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, earliestAvailableVisitDateTime). It listens for events from the cards (book visit, view details) and forwards these to the state manager for navigation handling.

Displays when searchNurseriesForVisit returns an empty array. Provides a message indicating that no nurseries match the current filters and encourages the user to adjust filters. Does not use or display any static nursery data. It remains in sync with the filter state so users understand which criteria led to zero results.

Represents a single nursery result within the results list. It displays the nursery name, district, distanceFromHomeKm, ratingAverage with ratingCount, baseMonthlyFee with currency, opensByTimeWeekday, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground indication, and earliestAvailableVisitDateTime if provided. It exposes actions for booking a visit and viewing details, emitting events with nurseryId when these actions are triggered.

Keeps the filter and sort state synchronized with the URL query parameters. On initialization or when the URL changes, it parses childAgeYears, maxDistanceKm, hasLargeOutdoorPlayground, maxMonthlyFee, minRating, and sortBy, and updates the state manager. When the filter or sort state changes inside the app, it updates the URL parameters accordingly without relying on localStorage.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with two main stacked bands inside a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background. Band 1 (top) is a hero-like control strip: a large, friendly results summary on the left and the sort control on the right, all inside a soft, rounded rectangle similar to the original slider area. Band 2 (below) is a two-column layout inside the same central content width: the left column is the filter sidebar and the right column is the results area (results summary context flows into it, followed by either the results list or the empty state). The state manager and URL sync wrap the entire page logically but are non-visual.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow with two main stacked bands inside a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background. Band 1 (top) is a hero-like control strip: a large, friendly results summary on the left and the sort control on the right, all inside a soft, rounded rectangle similar to the original slider area. Band 2 (below) is a two-column layout inside the same central content width: the left column is the filter sidebar and the right column is the results area (results summary context flows into it, followed by either the results list or the empty state). The state manager and URL sync wrap the entire page logically but are non-visual.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The fixed-width content container (roughly corresponding to a 12-column grid) is centered on the page with generous lateral whitespace. The top hero-like strip spans the full width of this container with substantial padding (24–32px) and rounded corners. Below it, the main two-column layout allocates about 38–40% of the width to the filter sidebar on the left and 60–62% to the results area on the right, echoing the mockup’s text-vs-illustration split but adapted for functional content. Vertical spacing between the top band and the two-column area follows the 48–64px section margin pattern, and individual components within each band follow 16–32px internal spacing.
layout_strategies.content_density: Controls and text are spaced generously with low-to-moderate density, mirroring the child-friendly editorial feel. The results summary uses a large, bold heading and a short, clear subline. Filter groups and result cards are padded (24–32px) with ample gaps (16–24px) between clusters, avoiding visual clutter. The design uses large, legible typography and comfortable line height, trading a bit of above-the-fold density for clarity and calm. The empty state in particular is intentionally airy, with a large illustration and minimal text.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow adapts the mockup’s loose Z-pattern. The user’s eye starts at the top-left of the hero band on the prominent results summary, then moves horizontally to the top-right where the sort control resides (analogous to the original slider arrows). From there, the eye drops diagonally down to the left column where the filter sidebar begins, and then sweeps across to the right column where the vertically stacked result cards form the main scanning path. The warm, card-based layout and playful accents (rounded badges, soft colors) guide attention without feeling rigid, with the right column’s cards acting as the primary reading track and the left filters as a steady, secondary anchor.

layout.overall_description: The Visit Search page sits inside a centered, fixed-width container over a soft, playful background that hints at watercolor textures and rounded, retro shapes. At the very top of this container, a wide, pill-cornered band stretches from left to right: on its left side, a large, friendly heading announces the visit search and shows how many nurseries match, while on its right, a compact but prominent sort selector floats like a neatly aligned control, echoing the original hero’s right-aligned slider arrows. A generous vertical gap below this band introduces the main content area, which is divided into two columns. The left column is a vertically scrolling filter panel framed within a softly colored card, with each filter group spaced apart like comfortable paragraphs. The right column is the primary content zone: it begins with a subtle continuation of the summary context and then flows into a column of rounded result cards, each one generously padded and separated by airy gaps that let each nursery profile breathe. When no results are found, the entire right column shifts into an illustrated empty state centered within that space, maintaining the warm, child-friendly tone. Throughout, the eye moves naturally from the top-left summary, across to the sort control, then down into the left filters and across the right-hand list, creating an easy Z-shaped path through a layout that feels modern in structure but soft and playful in personality.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is an invisible controller that conceptually wraps the entire Visit Search page. It lives at the top of the page hierarchy, spanning the full viewport width, but does not render any visual elements or occupy explicit height. All visible components (hero-like summary/sort band, filter sidebar, results area, empty state, and result cards) are nested within its logical scope. Spatially, it is best thought of as a full-page container that governs loading, error, and data states but does not change the visual layout or spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual root controller; occupies no actual layout space and does not affect positioning. It orchestrates which visual child components are shown (results list vs. empty state, etc.) without altering the two-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar forms the left column of the main two-column area beneath the top summary/sort band. It starts approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the hero-like band and aligns vertically with the left edge of the central content container. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 38–40% of the container width (about 4–5 columns of a 12-column grid), wrapped in a softly tinted, rounded-corner card that visually echoes the Üdvözlet text block in the mockup. Inside this card, filter groups are stacked from top to bottom with clear section headings, 16–24px gaps between groups, and internal padding of 24–32px on all sides. On tall pages, the filter bar appears as a stable vertical anchor: its top edge aligns with the bottom of the hero band, and it stretches downward as far as needed based on filter content. On desktop, it remains visually prominent yet secondary to the right-hand results by using slightly lighter colors and smaller typography than the main heading, while still feeling approachable and playful with rounded controls and pill-shaped checkboxes or tag filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a left sidebar within the central container. On larger screens it can use a sticky position so that its top edge remains just below the main navigation and hero band while the results column scrolls independently. On smaller screens, it collapses above the results into a full-width accordion or drawer but still preserves generous padding and section spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control sits in the top hero-like band, aligned to the right side to mirror the mockup’s slider arrows. It shares a common rounded, card-like background with the results summary but is visually segmented by spacing rather than hard borders. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 20–25% of the container width (about 3 columns on a 12-column grid) and is right-aligned with the overall content container, leaving a comfortable 24–32px gap between its left edge and the summary area. Vertically, it is centered within the height of the hero band, so that its label and dropdown align with the baseline of the summary’s subline, creating a cohesive horizontal row. The control itself is a pill-shaped dropdown or segmented toggle with about 8–12px internal padding around the label, maintaining the low-density feel; it does not stack multiple rows of controls here to keep the band clean and focused.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline, right-aligned element within the hero band. On wide screens it stays in the top-right of this band; on narrow screens it wraps beneath the summary, becoming a full-width control with extra top margin (16–24px) while still contained within the same rounded background section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary is the primary focal point of the top hero-like strip. It is positioned at the top-left of the central content container, beginning just below the site’s global header. It occupies approximately 70–75% of the container width (about 8–9 grid columns), leaving room on the right for the sort control. The summary sits inside a large, horizontally oriented rounded rectangle with a soft, warm background color, 24–32px padding on all sides, and ample whitespace above and below the text. The summary typically includes a prominent heading (e.g., ‘Visit Search’ or ‘X Municipal Nurseries Found’) and a shorter explanatory line beneath it. These two lines together take up roughly the upper 25–30% of the visible hero band, echoing the heading plus intro arrangement in the Üdvözlet section. The remaining vertical space within the band remains mostly open, with generous line height and breathing room to maintain the relaxed, child-friendly tone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, top-of-page content block. It defines the top of the main content flow and does not scroll independently; as the user scrolls, it moves off-screen with the rest of the content. It anchors the Z-pattern by drawing initial attention to the top-left before the eye traverses to the sort control and then down into the two-column area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the right-hand column of the main two-column area, directly opposite the filter bar and beginning on the same horizontal line as the top of the filter card. It is aligned with the right edge of the central content container and spans roughly 60–62% of its width (about 7–8 grid columns). The list itself is a vertical stack of nursery result cards, each card filling the full width of this right column. There is a consistent 16–24px vertical gap between cards, respecting the low-density, editorial-like feel. The top of the list may include a subtle row that reiterates the count or active filters in a smaller, calm text style, but this remains compact to avoid creating a second hero area. As the user scrolls, the list extends downward, forming the main scrolling content of the page, while the filter column remains visible or partially visible on larger screens. The list’s background remains the page background (light and warm), allowing the individual cards’ rounded white surfaces to pop forward visually.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable vertical column within the main content area. On large screens, it scrolls alongside a (potentially sticky) filter sidebar; on smaller screens, it becomes a full-width column beneath the filter controls. When the state manager indicates no results, this entire area is replaced by the empty state component in the same spatial slot.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state is positioned exactly where the results list would normally appear: in the right-hand column of the two-column layout, aligned to the right edge of the central container and spanning roughly 60–62% of its width. When shown, it overrides the list so that the right column becomes a single, tall card-like presentation. The content of the empty state is centered both horizontally within this column and vertically within the available viewport height (minus the space taken by the filter bar and any top padding), creating a calm, open feel. A large, playful illustration (e.g., children searching or looking at a map) sits near the top-middle of this area, roughly filling the upper half of the card’s vertical space. Below the illustration, a bold, child-friendly heading explains that no nurseries match the current filters, followed by a short paragraph suggesting adjustments. A primary action (such as ‘Clear filters’ or ‘Show all nurseries’) appears as a rounded, pill-shaped button beneath, with generous top margin (16–24px). Overall, the empty state uses substantial whitespace around its elements to feel warm, non-alarming, and spacious.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional content block that replaces the results list within the same right-column area when there are no matches. It scrolls with the page but is typically shorter than a full list, so it often appears mostly within the initial viewport after the hero band, minimizing the need to scroll to understand the situation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each nursery result card is a horizontally oriented, rounded rectangle that sits within the results list’s right column, spanning nearly the full width of that column with a small horizontal inset (e.g., 8–16px) on each side to emphasize separation from the background. The card has a soft, white or very light background, subtle shadow or border, and 24–32px of internal padding. Inside, content is arranged in a two-column layout: the left content zone (about 65–70% of the card width) contains the nursery name in a bold, friendly type, followed by key details like address, age groups, and visiting hours arranged in short lines or small bullet-like rows with generous 8–12px vertical spacing. Colored, pill-shaped badges (e.g., ‘Accessible’, ‘Meals included’) echo the numbered circles from the Üdvözlet section, placed near the top or beneath the name. The right content zone (about 30–35% of the card width) aligns to the card’s right edge and vertically centers key actions and summary info: a compact block for next available visit date and a primary, rounded ‘Book visit’ button. On taller cards, this right block aligns with the middle of the left content to create a stable horizontal feel. The vertical gap between successive cards in the list is about 16–24px, maintaining clear separation and a low-density rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeated, stackable content block within the results list column. It does not have special sticky or floating behavior; it scrolls naturally with the list. On smaller screens, its internal layout collapses to a single column: the right-side actions stack beneath the main details, with consistent vertical spacing and preserved padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component functions behind the scenes to synchronize filter selections, sort options, and pagination state with the URL. It does not render any visible UI and therefore does not occupy any explicit position within the visual grid. Conceptually, it sits alongside the state manager near the root of the page, influencing which results, empty states, and control states are displayed without altering the physical arrangement or spacing of those elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-height element. It has no impact on layout flow or component positioning; it only affects state and navigation (e.g., back/forward behavior, shareable links) in the background.

name: Visit Booking
title: Visit Booking
description: Page for selecting a visit date and time at a nursery, entering visitor details, creating a visit booking, and viewing the booking summary.

core_features: Load nursery context and available visit slots for a configurable date range using getVisitAvailability based on the nurseryId URL parameter.

Allow the user to pick a visit date via a calendar-style selector, including helpers to jump to the next occurrence of a selected weekday (for example, the next Wednesday), constrained by availableSlots from getVisitAvailability.
Display and allow selection of visit time slots (start and end times) that are available for the chosen date using availableSlots from getVisitAvailability.
Collect visitor details (parentName, parentPhone, reminderMethod) in a form tied to the selected time slot.
Create and confirm a visit booking by calling createVisitBooking using the selected nursery, slot, date/time, and visitor details, then expose the resulting VisitBooking for display.

Display a booking summary (including nursery info, date, start/end time, and reminder method) using either the booking from createVisitBooking or data from getVisitBookingSummary when visitBookingId is present in the URL.
Provide a clear end to the flow with a "Finish"/"Done" action that navigates back to visits.html.
user_workflows: New visit booking with preselected date (from visits.html): 1) User arrives at visit_booking.html with nurseryId and visitDate in the URL. 2) On load, the page calls getVisitAvailability with nurseryId and a date range that includes visitDate. 3) The nursery context is shown using the nursery returned by getVisitAvailability. 4) The visit date selector highlights visitDate and filters availableSlots to that date. 5) The user optionally uses weekday shortcuts (for example, selecting the next occurrence of a weekday) to change the date; if the new date is outside the currently loaded range, the page calls getVisitAvailability again for the new range. 6) The user chooses one available time slot for the selected date. 7) The user fills in parentName, parentPhone, and selects reminderMethod in the visitor details form. 8) The user submits the form; the page calls createVisitBooking with nurseryId, visitSlotId (from the chosen slot), visitDate, startTime, endTime, parentName, parentPhone, and reminderMethod. 9) On success, the page stores the returned booking in state, optionally updates the URL to include visitBookingId = booking.id, and switches to the booking summary view. 10) The user reviews the summary and clicks "Finish"/"Done" to navigate to visits.html.

New visit booking without preselected date (from nursery_detail.html or favorites.html): 1) User arrives at visit_booking.html with nurseryId in the URL and no visitDate. 2) On load, the page calls getVisitAvailability with nurseryId and a default future date range. 3) Nursery context is displayed. 4) The visit date selector initially focuses on a default date (e.g., today or the earliest date with availableSlots) but does not force a selection until the user chooses one. 5) The user selects a date by clicking in the calendar or using a weekday shortcut (for example, the next Wednesday button computes the next matching date that has availableSlots). 6) Available time slots for the selected date are listed, and the user selects one. 7) The user completes the visitor details form. 8) The user submits the booking, triggering createVisitBooking. 9) On success, the page transitions to the booking summary view as above. 10) The user finishes and is redirected to visits.html.

Viewing an existing booking summary: 1) User arrives at visit_booking.html with visitBookingId in the URL (nurseryId and visitDate may or may not be present but are not required). 2) On load, the page detects visitBookingId and calls getVisitBookingSummary with that value. 3) The page displays the booking summary view using the returned booking and nursery without exposing editable booking controls (no date/slot selection or visitor details form, or rendered in read-only mode). 4) The user can review the booking details and then click "Finish"/"Done" to return to visits.html.

Adjusting date range and handling unavailable days: 1) User changes the selected date or uses weekday shortcuts to find a suitable day. 2) If the chosen date falls within the currently loaded date range, the page reuses the in-memory availableSlots from the last getVisitAvailability call and filters them by date. 3) If the chosen date is outside the loaded range, the page issues a new getVisitAvailability call with a date range that includes the requested date. 4) If no availableSlots exist for the selected date, the page displays a functional message (derived from state, not hardcoded times) explaining that no slots are available for that date and prompts the user to select another date.

Completing the flow: 1) After viewing the booking summary (either directly via visitBookingId or immediately after creation), the user clicks "Finish"/"Done". 2) The page navigates to visits.html using a standard link or redirect, without passing booking details through localStorage; any needed context is either not required or handled by visits.html via its own logic.
interactions: On page load, parse the URL for nurseryId, visitDate, and visitBookingId. If visitBookingId is present, load summary mode via getVisitBookingSummary; otherwise, if nurseryId is present, load availability via getVisitAvailability.
On successful getVisitAvailability response, update the page state with nursery and availableSlots, and propagate nursery to contextual components.
On failure of getVisitAvailability or getVisitBookingSummary, show an error state and provide a retry action that re-invokes the respective interface.
When the user clicks on a date in the visit date selector, update the selectedDate state and filter availableSlots to those matching that date; update the time slot list accordingly.
