It does not occupy a large portion of the viewport—typically no more than 10–15% of vertical height—and it leaves ample space above and below so the layout stays calm and not cluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and transient: appears in the normal document flow either just above or inside the registration form card, pushing content down when visible. It scrolls with the page and disappears or collapses when not needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page state manager has no visible UI of its own. It orchestrates the visibility and states of other components—triggering the data loader overlay during fetch operations, controlling when the registration feedback notification appears, and ensuring the hero and detail cards render with the correct data. As such, it does not occupy any space in the visual layout and does not alter the spatial arrangement of the other components beyond causing them to appear, hide, or update.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non‑visual and non‑spatial: it exists purely in the application logic layer and does not render any element that takes up layout space.

name: Activity Registration Summary
title: Activity Registration Summary
description: Shows the current list of selected activities for municipal nursery and daycare centers, allows editing child details, removing activities, and submitting the full registration in one step.

core_features: Load the current activity registration list and grouped activities by center using getActivityRegistrationSummary when the page initializes and after any modifications.
Display child registration details (child name and number of children) bound to ActivityRegistrationList, and allow users to update them via updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails.
List all activities in the registration list grouped by ActivityCenter, showing activity date, time, age range, and price for each Activity.
Allow users to remove individual activities from the list by calling removeActivityFromRegistrationList with the selected ActivityRegistrationItem ID and then refreshing the summary.

Calculate and display a running total of activities and total estimated price based on Activity.pricePerSession and ActivityRegistrationList.numChildren, updating whenever the list or child details change.
Submit the entire registration list in one step via submitActivityRegistrationList, then show a confirmation state using the returned message, totalActivities, totalPrice, and currency.

Provide navigation back to the activities browsing page (activities.html) with age range and month parameters derived from the first activity in the list, and back to the home page (index.html) after successful submission.
user_workflows: Review current registration list: (1) User navigates to activity_registration.html (e.g., via a “View registration list” link); (2) Page calls getActivityRegistrationSummary; (3) The child details section shows the current childName and numChildren; (4) The activities list section shows all ActivityRegistrationItems grouped by ActivityCenter; (5) The price summary section computes totals from the loaded data.

Edit child details: (1) User clicks an Edit button in the child details section; (2) The section enters edit mode, allowing childName and numChildren fields to be changed; (3) User submits the form; (4) Page validates the input (e.g., non-empty childName, numChildren is a positive number); (5) On success, page calls updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails; (6) On a successful response, the updated ActivityRegistrationList is bound back into the page state; (7) Dependent components (activities list labels and price summary) update automatically.

Remove an activity from the registration list: (1) In the grouped activities list, user clicks a Remove button for a specific activity row; (2) Page calls removeActivityFromRegistrationList with the corresponding ActivityRegistrationItem.id; (3) If the response indicates success, page triggers a refresh by calling getActivityRegistrationSummary again; (4) If remainingItemsCount is zero, the activities section switches to an empty state and the submit button is disabled; (5) Price summary recalculates based on the refreshed data.

Add more activities from the summary page: (1) When at least one activity exists in the list, the page derives ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and month from the first Activity in the list; (2) User clicks an Add more activities link; (3) Page navigates to activities.html with URL parameters ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and month populated from that first Activity; (4) User can continue browsing and adding activities; (5) Returning to activity_registration.html shows the updated registration list via getActivityRegistrationSummary.

Submit the registration list: (1) User reviews child details, activities, and price summary; (2) The submit button is enabled only if there is at least one ActivityRegistrationItem, childName is valid, numChildren is a valid positive number, and the registrationList.status is in_progress; (3) User clicks the Submit registration button; (4) Page calls submitActivityRegistrationList; (5) While waiting, the button shows a loading state and is disabled; (6) On success, the returned registrationList (now with status submitted) and totals are stored in page state; (7) The page URL is updated (e.g., ?status=submitted) and the UI switches to a confirmation view that is read-only; (8) User can then click Back to home to navigate to index.html or Browse more activities to go to activities.html with derived parameters.

Handle pre-submitted or empty lists: (1) If getActivityRegistrationSummary returns a registrationList with status submitted, the page immediately shows the confirmation view instead of an editable summary; (2) If the registration list contains no ActivityRegistrationItems (e.g., remainingItemsCount is zero after a removal), the activities section shows an empty state with guidance and a link to activities.html (with parameters only if derivable from the first item, otherwise without parameters), and the submit button is disabled.
interactions: On page load, a load event triggers getActivityRegistrationSummary. The response populates the ActivityRegistrationList and activitiesByCenter, which in turn populate the child details section, activities grouped list, and price summary panel.
Clicking the Edit child details button toggles the child details component into edit mode, enabling input fields but not calling any API yet.

Submitting the child details form triggers validation and then calls updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails with childName and numChildren. On a successful response, the child details component exits edit mode and shows the updated values; the price summary is recomputed.
Clicking Cancel in the child details edit form discards unsaved changes and returns to the last saved state from ActivityRegistrationList without calling any API.

Clicking Remove on an activity row calls removeActivityFromRegistrationList with the relevant registrationItemId. During the request, the row’s Remove button is disabled. On success, the list is reloaded with getActivityRegistrationSummary and the UI updates accordingly.

Clicking Add more activities navigates to activities.html. If at least one Activity exists, the page appends ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and month URL parameters derived from the first Activity’s ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and date; if none exist, navigation to activities.html occurs without those parameters.

Clicking Submit registration triggers submitActivityRegistrationList, disables the button, and shows a loading indicator. On success, the page updates to a confirmation state and updates the URL (e.g., adds status=submitted). On error, an error notification is shown and the button is re-enabled without leaving the page.

In confirmation state, clicking Back to home navigates to index.html, and clicking Browse more activities navigates to activities.html with the same logic for deriving URL parameters from the first Activity in the (submitted) list.

If the URL includes a scrollTo parameter, after the initial data load the page scrolls to the referenced section (for example, child-details or activities-list) and optionally highlights it briefly without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page does not receive a specific registration list ID via the URL; instead, all APIs operate on the current user's single ActivityRegistrationList managed on the backend. URL parameters are used only to control presentation state and outgoing navigation: (1) On load, the page always calls getActivityRegistrationSummary to retrieve the current registrationList and activitiesByCenter. (2) If the registrationList returned has status submitted, the page immediately renders in confirmation mode regardless of URL parameters. If status is in_progress, the page renders in editable mode. (3) After a successful call to submitActivityRegistrationList, the page updates the browser URL (using query parameters, such as ?status=submitted and optionally scrollTo=confirmation) so that reloading the page will show the confirmation state again (because getActivityRegistrationSummary then returns the submitted list). The URL status parameter is treated as advisory; the actual mode is determined by registrationList.status. (4) Optional URL parameter scrollTo is read after the initial data load; if present and matching a known section identifier, the page scrolls to that component and applies a temporary focus/highlight. This parameter does not affect API calls. (5) Navigation to activities.html uses URL parameters ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and month when these can be derived from the first Activity in activitiesByCenter; if no activities are present, navigation occurs without those parameters to avoid relying on nonexistent data. (6) No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used; all authoritative state (child details, items, status) comes from the four assigned interfaces and is reflected purely in in-memory page state and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by loading the current activity registration summary and exposes a method to refresh this data after updates or removals.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getActivityRegistrationSummary with no parameters, then store registrationList and activitiesByCenter in page state.

onRefreshRequested: Re-call getActivityRegistrationSummary after successful updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails, removeActivityFromRegistrationList, or submitActivityRegistrationList, and propagate the updated data to dependent components.

components.functionality: Displays and edits the child details (childName and numChildren) used for all activities in the registration list.
event_handlers: onEnterEditModeClick: Switch the component to edit mode, copying the current childName and numChildren from ActivityRegistrationList into editable form fields.
onFieldChange: Update the component's local form state as the user types, without calling any APIs.

onSubmit: Validate inputs (e.g., childName not empty, numChildren is a positive number); if valid, call updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails with the edited childName and numChildren; on success, update the bound ActivityRegistrationList instance in page state and exit edit mode; then notify price-summary-panel and other components that registrationList has changed.
onCancelEditClick: Discard any unsaved changes by resetting fields from the latest ActivityRegistrationList values and exit edit mode without calling updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails.

components.functionality: Shows all activities in the registration list grouped by center, with per-activity details and an option to remove items before submission.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When registration-summary-init provides new activitiesByCenter data, render each group with center information and its associated activities. Use ActivityRegistrationList.numChildren to annotate rows (for example, by indicating that each activity applies to that number of children) and to inform price calculations in price-summary-panel.
onToggleCenterGroup: Allow the user to expand or collapse the list of activities under each ActivityCenter without affecting backend data.

onRemoveActivityClick: For a given activity row, retrieve the corresponding ActivityRegistrationItem.id and call removeActivityFromRegistrationList with registrationItemId; on success and success=true, trigger registration-summary-init.onRefreshRequested so the list and totals update from the refreshed summary.

onEmptyListDetected: If the loaded activitiesByCenter is empty or removeActivityFromRegistrationList returns remainingItemsCount = 0, notify empty-registration-state to show the empty UI and ensure submit-registration-action is disabled.

components.functionality: Displays a computed overview of total activities and estimated total price for the current registration list, and after submission shows the confirmed totals returned by submitActivityRegistrationList.
event_handlers: onDataChange: When registration-summary-init or child-details-editor updates ActivityRegistrationList or activitiesByCenter, recompute the total number of activities and the estimated total price using in-memory data (aggregating Activity.pricePerSession across all ActivityRegistrationItems and multiplying appropriately by ActivityRegistrationList.numChildren). Use any available currency from the Activity records, handling the case where multiple currencies may appear by following a consistent client-side rule.

onSubmissionSuccess: After submitActivityRegistrationList returns, override the locally computed totals with totalActivities, totalPrice, and currency from the API response to match the authoritative backend calculation, and mark the panel as showing confirmed values rather than estimates.

components.functionality: Provides the primary control for submitting the entire activity registration list in one action.
event_handlers: onStateEvaluation: Enable or disable the Submit registration button based on current page state: it is disabled if registrationList.status is submitted, if no ActivityRegistrationItems are present (as signaled from activities-grouped-summary or empty-registration-state), if childName or numChildren are invalid, or while a submission is in progress.

onClick: When the button is enabled and clicked, start a loading state and call submitActivityRegistrationList with no parameters; on success, update the bound ActivityRegistrationList to the returned version (with status submitted), notify submission-confirmation-panel and price-summary-panel of the new data, and update the URL to include a status=submitted parameter (and optionally scrollTo=confirmation); on error, stop the loading state, display an error via a notification component, and leave the page in editable state.
onPostSubmitDisable: After a successful submission, keep the button disabled so the registration list cannot be resubmitted from this page.

components.functionality: Shows an informative empty state when there are no activities in the registration list and guides the user to add activities.
event_handlers: onEmptyDetected: When activities-grouped-summary signals that there are zero ActivityRegistrationItems (either on initial load or after removals), display an empty state component instead of the grouped list and ensure submit-registration-action is disabled.

onAddActivitiesClick: Navigate to activities.html. If a first Activity is available in the current page state (e.g., immediately after a reload where activitiesByCenter is not empty), append URL parameters ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and month derived from that Activity. If no Activity data is available, navigate without those parameters to avoid using unavailable values.

components.functionality: Provides a persistent link on the summary page for users to return to the activities browsing page to add more items.
event_handlers: onClick: Determine whether any Activity objects exist in the current activitiesByCenter structure; if yes, derive ageMinYears and ageMaxYears from the first Activity, and derive the month from that Activity.date; then navigate to activities.html with these values as URL parameters; if not, navigate to activities.html without additional parameters.

components.functionality: Shows a confirmation message and summary after the registration list has been successfully submitted and makes the page read-only.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When ActivityRegistrationList.status is submitted (either from initial getActivityRegistrationSummary or after submitActivityRegistrationList), display the confirmation panel using information from the latest submitActivityRegistrationList response (message, totalActivities, totalPrice, currency, and childName). Hide or disable editing controls such as child-details-editor edit actions, remove buttons in activities-grouped-summary, and submit-registration-action.
onBackToHomeClick: Navigate to index.html with no additional parameters.
onBrowseMoreActivitiesClick: Navigate to activities.html using the same logic as add-more-activities-link for deriving ageMinYears, ageMaxYears, and month from the first Activity when available.

onUrlStatusCheck: If the URL contains status=submitted but registrationList.status is still in_progress after getActivityRegistrationSummary, prioritize the backend status and keep the panel hidden, treating the list as still editable.

components.functionality: Displays error messages resulting from API failures or validation issues on this page.
event_handlers: onApiError: When any of the interfaces (getActivityRegistrationSummary, updateActivityRegistrationChildDetails, removeActivityFromRegistrationList, submitActivityRegistrationList) returns an error, show a contextual error message and allow the user to retry the action or continue interacting with other components.
onDismiss: Allow the user to close the notification. Dismissing a notification does not alter any backend data and does not trigger additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear top-to-bottom flow with a clear, warm page header at the top, followed by the main registration content inside a rounded, card-like container. The main content area follows a two-column layout similar to the mockup’s ‘Üdvözlet’ section: left column for text-heavy elements (child details form + activities list), right column for a compact visual summary and main action (price summary + submit). State-based components (empty state, confirmation panel, loader, errors) either replace or overlay this main container while preserving the same centered, fixed-width structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear top-to-bottom flow with a clear, warm page header at the top, followed by the main registration content inside a rounded, card-like container. The main content area follows a two-column layout similar to the mockup’s ‘Üdvözlet’ section: left column for text-heavy elements (child details form + activities list), right column for a compact visual summary and main action (price summary + submit). State-based components (empty state, confirmation panel, loader, errors) either replace or overlay this main container while preserving the same centered, fixed-width structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Place all content inside a centered fixed-width container (approx. 12-column grid) with fluid background color or pattern. Allocate about 55–60% of the inner width to the left column (forms and lists) and 40–45% to the right column (summary, actions), mirroring the text/illustration balance from the mockup. Hero-like header and major panels sit inside rounded rectangles with 24–32px internal padding. Vertical gaps between sections are around 48–64px; within sections, headings, forms, and lists are separated by 16–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderately low content density: larger, readable font sizes; generous line height; ample white space between form fields, list groups, and panels. Lists of activities are grouped into visually distinct blocks with clear breathing room. The right-hand price summary is concise and visually calm, acting like the illustration space in the mockup, balancing the denser left column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye in a loose Z-pattern: start with the page title at the top-left inside a rounded header band, then across to any supporting elements on the right (e.g., a brief helper text or icon). From there, drop down to the left column for child details and the grouped activity list, scanning downward through sections. The eye is periodically pulled to the right column where the price summary and the prominent submit button live, acting as a secondary focal area similar to the illustration in the mockup. Error and confirmation messages appear near the top of the container, while a floating scroll-to-top control (outside this component list) would quietly anchor the bottom-right of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The page sits within a soft, warm background, with a centered, fixed-width content container that feels like a large rounded card floating on the page. At the very top of this container is a friendly, hero-like header band with the page title “Activity Registration Summary” and a short explanatory line, following the mockup’s wide, rounded slider look but without complex controls. Immediately below, the main registration area adopts the two-column pattern from the ‘Üdvözlet’ section: the left 60% of the width is dedicated to a stacked flow of the child details form followed by the grouped list of selected activities, each separated by generous vertical spacing. The right 40% forms a tall, cozy summary panel showing total price, key counts, and the main submission button, visually echoing the role of the illustration in the mockup—less text, more visual calm, and a clear call to action. When there are no selected activities, a wide empty-state panel replaces the two-column layout with a side-by-side message and illustration, again mimicking the welcome section’s composition. Error notifications and submission confirmations appear as rounded strips or panels toward the top of the content stack, gently shifting the rest of the layout downward. A loader can overlay the main card when needed, dimming it slightly while keeping the overall structure intact. The result is a simple, linear scroll with clear, separated sections, large typography, and plenty of whitespace, all framed by soft, rounded shapes that feel appropriate for a municipal nursery context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay centered over the main rounded content container (including both columns), but inside the overall page boundaries. It covers roughly 90–95% of the container’s width and extends from just below the page header band down to the lower edge of the main content, following its rounded corners so the shape still feels consistent with the card-like layout. A circular spinner or playful loading illustration sits at the vertical and horizontal center of this overlay, with a brief loading label beneath it. The background content (child details, activity list, price panel, buttons) remains faintly visible but visually subdued, signaling that the layout is in place but temporarily inactive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-container overlay positioned absolutely within the main content region; it appears and disappears on top of existing components without altering their layout, intercepting interactions while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The child details form occupies the top portion of the left column within the main two-column area, starting directly below any error or confirmation notifications and the header band. It spans approximately 55–60% of the content container’s width, aligned to the left, with 24–32px of padding on all sides inside a softly rounded card. The form’s heading sits at the top-left of this card with about 16–24px of space below it before the fields begin. Form fields are laid out in a vertical stack with comfortable 16–24px gaps between them, ensuring an airy feel. The bottom of the form maintains about 24px of padding before the card’s rounded edge, and there is a 32–48px vertical gap between the bottom of this form card and the top of the activities-grouped-summary card below, maintaining the section separation seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; scrolls with the page as part of the main vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The grouped activities summary sits directly beneath the child details form in the left column, maintaining the same column width (about 55–60% of the container) and left alignment. It is wrapped in its own rounded panel with 24–32px internal padding. A section title, such as “Selected Activities,” appears at the top-left of this card, with 16–24px spacing below before the list begins. Activities are organized into visually separated groups: each group heading appears on the left with a slightly bolder style and about 8–16px padding above and below, and the activities under it form a vertical list with comfortable 12–16px spacing between items. Between groups, there is a larger 24px gap, echoing the numbered paragraphs’ vertical rhythm in the mockup. The far left of the card can include small colored badges or icons for each group, occupying a narrow 1–2 column-like strip, with the activity details taking up the remaining width—an echo of the numbered circles and text relationship. The card ends with a 24–32px bottom padding before transition to any link or next section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; grows vertically as activities are added, pushing content below it downward in the scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price summary panel is positioned at the top of the right column, aligned with the top of the child details editor on the left whenever possible. It occupies about 40–45% of the container width, with its left edge aligned to the vertical centerline between the two columns. The panel is a vertically oriented, softly rounded card with 24–32px internal padding, slightly contrasting background color, and minimal text to keep it visually light—functionally mirroring the illustrative block on the right in the mockup. At the top of this card, a concise heading like “Summary” appears, followed by key figures (total price, number of activities, schedule notes) arranged in a simple vertical stack with 8–16px spacing. A dividing line or subtle background band can separate the informational section from the action area below. The main submit button is placed near the bottom of this panel (see submit-registration-action), with 16–24px top margin from the last summary line and 24px bottom padding below the button to maintain breathing room.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column on small viewports; on taller screens and desktops, it can behave as a sticky panel that remains visible as the user scrolls through a longer activities list on the left, sticking to the top offset of the main container once reached.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit registration action is rendered as a large, primary button anchored within the lower portion of the price-summary-panel on the right column. It spans roughly 80–90% of the width of the summary panel, centered horizontally, with 12–16px internal vertical padding and rounded corners that echo the overall design. There is a generous 16–24px gap above the button separating it from the last piece of price information, and at least 24px of padding below the button to the bottom edge of the panel, preserving the open feel seen in the mockup. Visually, this button becomes the main call-to-action focal point on the right side of the screen, balancing the denser text on the left. On very narrow screens (e.g., mobile), the button can relocate to below the price summary content, spanning nearly the full width of the container while still maintaining 16–24px separation from adjacent elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored inside the price-summary-panel; participates in any sticky behavior of that panel on larger screens, remaining visible as part of the right column summary.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When there are no selected activities, the empty state replaces the two-column child-details + activities + price summary layout. In this state, a single, wide rounded card is centered in the container beneath the header band, spanning about 80–90% of the content width. Inside this card, the layout mirrors the mockup’s ‘Üdvözlet’ section: the left 55–60% contains a friendly heading such as “No activities selected yet,” followed by a short, bold introductory sentence and one or two brief explanatory paragraphs. The right 40–45% is reserved for a large, playful illustration (e.g., children playing or building blocks) that echoes the warm, watercolor style. There is generous spacing—24–32px—between the heading and body text, and similar vertical separation between paragraphs, maintaining a low-density, editorial feel. The primary link or button for adding activities (see add-more-activities-link) is placed under the text block on the left with 24px top margin, aligning visually with the numbered points area from the mockup. The illustration has ample whitespace around it, not touching the card edges, to keep the composition light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static replacement for the main two-column section; when active, it pushes other components (like confirmation panels or notifications) above or below it in the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the standard state (when there are selected activities), the add-more-activities link appears below the activities-grouped-summary card in the left column. It is left-aligned with the card above and sits about 16–24px beneath it, occupying only the natural width of its label, with a small icon (e.g., a plus sign) to its left. The surrounding whitespace keeps it light and non-intrusive, acting as a tertiary call to action. In the empty state, this same link or button appears prominently below the explanatory text on the left side of the empty-registration-state card, with 24px spacing above, making it the main next-step action while still preserving visual simplicity. In both contexts, it does not disrupt the two-column balance and remains clearly associated with the activities area on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element following the activities content or empty state text; scrolls naturally with the page without sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After a successful submission, the confirmation panel appears near the top of the main content container, just below any persistent site header and above the child-details and activities section. It spans the full width of the internal container (similar to the hero-like header) and is presented as a rounded rectangle with 24–32px padding and a soft, positive background color. Inside, the layout loosely echoes the welcome section: on the left 60% of the panel sits a bold confirmation headline (e.g., “Registration Submitted!”) and one or two short, readable lines explaining what happens next, spaced with 12–16px gaps. The right 40% can contain a small confirmation illustration or icon cluster, aligned right and vertically centered in the panel, keeping things playful and consistent with the visual DNA. Below this panel, a 32–48px gap separates it from the main two-column layout, which remains visible and can show the submitted details in a read-only or lightly editable form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, but inserted high in the vertical stack; it pushes the main content downward and remains visible only while its state is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notification appears as a slim but noticeable bar or small rounded panel at the very top of the content stack, directly under any global site navigation and above the header band or main two-column card. It spans the full width of the content container with 16–24px internal vertical padding, using a warm, accessible warning color. The error icon and message text are aligned horizontally from left to right, with the icon on the far left and text following, maintaining comfortable 8–12px gaps. A dismiss icon (e.g., an ‘x’) sits on the far right. Vertically, there is about 16–24px spacing between the bottom of this notification and the next element (either the page header band or the confirmation panel), so it doesn’t feel cramped. Its height is modest, keeping the layout light while still clearly signaling an issue.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears at the top of the content area and can behave as a temporary sticky element: on scroll, it may remain fixed just below the global header until dismissed or until the user scrolls past an initial threshold, then scroll away with the content.

name: Fees & Subsidies Overview
title: Fees & Subsidies
description: Displays municipal nursery fees and subsidy information using dynamic content, and guides users to the fee calculator and application pages.

core_features: Load fee overview content (general explanation, example fees, subsidies summary) using getFeesOverviewContent on page initialization.
Display a general fees overview section bound to the overviewText returned by getFeesOverviewContent.
Display an example fees section that lists fee examples from exampleFees returned by getFeesOverviewContent.
Display a subsidies section that shows the subsidiesSummary returned by getFeesOverviewContent.
Provide navigation controls (e.g., tabs or links) to jump between the Overview, Example Fees, and Subsidies sections, synchronized with a URL query parameter for the active section.
Provide a prominent call-to-action to open the interactive fee calculator (navigating to fee_calculator.html with context in URL parameters).
Provide a prominent call-to-action to start an application (navigating to application.html with context in URL parameters).
Handle loading and error states for getFeesOverviewContent and display appropriate status feedback to the user without hardcoding fee data.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Understand general fees then open calculator: 1) User navigates to fees.html from the main navigation or footer. 2) On page load, the system calls getFeesOverviewContent and shows a loading state. 3) When data is received, the Overview section displays overviewText, and the Example Fees and Subsidies sections become available via navigation controls. 4) User reads the Overview section to understand how fees generally work. 5) User either clicks the navigation control for Example Fees to see exampleFees, or stays in Overview. 6) When ready to estimate their own situation, the user clicks the "Fee calculator" call-to-action. 7) The system navigates to fee_calculator.html, including URL parameters indicating source=fees_overview and any currently selected example index if applicable.

Workflow 2 – Focus on subsidies then start an application: 1) User arrives at fees.html with a URL parameter section=subsidies (for example from a deep link in another page). 2) On page load, getFeesOverviewContent is called. 3) Once data is loaded, the Subsidies section is automatically brought into focus based on the section URL parameter, and subsidiesSummary is displayed. 4) User reads the subsidies information and may switch to Overview or Example Fees via navigation controls, which update the section URL parameter. 5) After becoming comfortable with the expected fee range and subsidies, the user clicks the "Start an application" call-to-action. 6) The system navigates to application.html with URL parameters indicating source=fees_overview and optionally including the current section and any selected example index as context.

Workflow 3 – Share or revisit a specific section or example: 1) User navigates between sections (Overview, Example Fees, Subsidies) using the section navigation controls; each click updates the section query parameter in the URL. 2) In the Example Fees section, user selects one of the exampleFees items, which updates an exampleIndex query parameter in the URL. 3) The user copies the URL from the browser to share with another parent or to bookmark it. 4) When someone opens this URL later, the page calls getFeesOverviewContent, then uses the section and exampleIndex query parameters to re-open the same section and highlight or focus the same example fee item without needing any hardcoded fee values.
interactions: On page load, the fees-overview-data-loader component automatically calls getFeesOverviewContent, manages loading and error states, and exposes overviewText, exampleFees, and subsidiesSummary to other components.

Clicking a section navigation control (e.g., Overview, Example Fees, Subsidies) updates the section query parameter in the URL (e.g., fees.html?section=examples) and triggers scrolling or focus to the corresponding section.

When the URL changes (for example, user manually edits the section query parameter or opens a shared link), the section navigation component reads the section parameter and adjusts the active section state accordingly.
In the Example Fees section, clicking on an example fee item updates the exampleIndex query parameter in the URL to the index of the selected example and updates the internal selectedExample state.

If the user navigates directly to fees.html with pre-existing query parameters (section, exampleIndex, from, next), the page parses these parameters after getFeesOverviewContent resolves and initializes the UI state (active section, selected example) to match.

Clicking the "Fee calculator" call-to-action in the CTA bar triggers navigation to fee_calculator.html with query parameters including source=fees_overview, the current section value, and the selected exampleIndex (if any) so the calculator can optionally preconfigure itself based on the example.

Clicking the "Start an application" call-to-action triggers navigation to application.html with query parameters including source=fees_overview and optionally the current section and selected exampleIndex so the application flow can show context-sensitive guidance.

If getFeesOverviewContent returns an error or fails, the status-display component shows an error state and offers a non-data-dependent option such as a generic retry action that recalls getFeesOverviewContent.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL query parameters to control and restore UI state without passing parameters into getFeesOverviewContent. The section parameter controls which major content section is active: for example, section can equal 'overview', 'examples', or 'subsidies'. On initial load and on any URL change, the section-navigation component reads the section parameter; if it is missing or unrecognized, it falls back to a default section without setting any fee-related defaults. The exampleIndex parameter controls which item in the exampleFees array is considered 'selected' in the Example Fees section. After getFeesOverviewContent loads, the example-fees-section interprets exampleIndex as a zero-based or one-based index (consistently defined in implementation) within the dynamically returned exampleFees collection; if the index is out of range or missing, no item is selected by default. Optional parameters such as from and next are used for navigation context only: from may indicate which previous page the user came from (e.g., 'fee_calculator' or 'application'), and next can indicate a recommended next action (e.g., 'application') to highlight in the CTA bar. These context parameters never alter the fee data itself; they only affect UI emphasis (for instance, which CTA is visually prominent or which section is scrolled into view). All page state that needs to be shareable or restorable (active section, selected example) is derived solely from these query parameters and the latest result of getFeesOverviewContent.

components.functionality: On page initialization, calls getFeesOverviewContent once to retrieve overviewText, exampleFees, and subsidiesSummary, manages loading and error states, and exposes the loaded data to child components via a shared state or context.

Controls which main content section (Overview, Example Fees, Subsidies) is active based on the section query parameter and allows the user to switch sections, updating the URL accordingly and coordinating with the rest of the page.
Displays the general description of how nursery and daycare fees work, binding directly to overviewText from getFeesOverviewContent and showing or hiding itself depending on the active section state.

Renders the list of example fee scenarios using the exampleFees array from getFeesOverviewContent, allows selecting a specific example to be used as context, and synchronizes the selected example with the exampleIndex query parameter.

Shows the summary explanation of available subsidies, discounts, and eligibility criteria using subsidiesSummary from getFeesOverviewContent and participates in section switching based on the section query parameter.

Provides two primary actions: open the fee calculator and start an application. It reads the current section and exampleIndex from the URL to include as context when navigating to fee_calculator.html or application.html, and may adjust which button is emphasized based on a next query parameter.
Shows the current loading, success, or error state for the getFeesOverviewContent request and, in case of failure, offers a generic retry interaction without hardcoding any fee-related data.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered, fixed-width content container (~10–11 of 12 grid columns) on a warm, soft-toned background. At the very top of the content area sits a slim status strip, followed by a large, welcoming overview section that visually acts as the page's hero. Directly beneath this, a pill-style section navigation bar lets users jump between the main fee content areas. Below that, content stacks in clear sections: first a list of fee examples, then a more detailed subsidies explanation, and finally a prominent CTA bar near the bottom that leads to the calculator and application pages. A data-loader overlays this central column when content is being fetched, without disrupting the underlying structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom flow inside a centered, fixed-width content container (~10–11 of 12 grid columns) on a warm, soft-toned background. At the very top of the content area sits a slim status strip, followed by a large, welcoming overview section that visually acts as the page's hero. Directly beneath this, a pill-style section navigation bar lets users jump between the main fee content areas. Below that, content stacks in clear sections: first a list of fee examples, then a more detailed subsidies explanation, and finally a prominent CTA bar near the bottom that leads to the calculator and application pages. A data-loader overlays this central column when content is being fetched, without disrupting the underlying structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content column is centered and constrained, with generous side margins on large screens and a fluid layout on smaller ones. Most sections follow a two-column grid similar to the mockup: roughly 55–60% width for left-side text and 40–45% for right-side supporting visuals or structured content. The top overview section occupies most of the first viewport height with large typography and illustration. The fee examples section uses the majority of the width for the list itself, with a narrow left gutter for badges, echoing the mockup's numbered circles. Vertical spacing between major sections is in the 48–64px range, and internal section padding is around 24–32px on all sides, with 16–24px gaps between headings, paragraphs, and list items.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is kept moderately low. Large, friendly type, generous line height, and plenty of breathing room around blocks mirror the relaxed, child-friendly tone of the mockup. Lists of fees and examples are broken into short, scannable chunks, each with clear separation and visual anchors (badges, icons, or mini-cards) rather than dense tables. Explanatory text about subsidies is grouped into 2–4 concise blocks instead of long continuous paragraphs. The data-loader and status display are visually light so they inform without cluttering.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a gentle Z-pattern within each viewport. The eye first lands on the status strip near the top-left of the content area, then moves down into the large overview heading and introductory copy. From there, it is pulled to the right-side illustration in the overview section. Below, the eye crosses back to the section navigation bar, then drops into the left-aligned headings of the fee examples, scanning downward through numbered badges and their descriptions. The right sides of sections support this with lighter visual elements or grouped cards that periodically catch attention without overpowering the main text. The CTA bar at the bottom and a separate floating scroll-to-top control (outside this component list) gently anchor the lower-right portion of the viewport, completing the vertical journey.

layout.overall_description: Inside a warm, softly tinted page background, a centered content column sits within a fixed-width container that feels like a friendly editorial page rather than a rigid dashboard. At the top of this column, a slim, rounded status strip quietly announces the current fee year or update state. Immediately beneath, a large, rounded-rectangle overview panel introduces nursery fees and subsidies in a two-column layout: on the left, a bold, welcoming headline and short, friendly paragraphs; on the right, a watercolor-style illustration of children, savings, or a small house, echoing the original mockup’s illustrated scene. This overview section occupies much of the initial viewport, with generous padding and air around the text. Just below it, a pill-shaped navigation bar stretches across the middle of the column, offering clear tabs for “Overview,” “Examples,” and “Subsidies” and subtly echoing the hero-slider controls from the mockup. As the user scrolls, they move into a spacious fee examples section where each example is laid out as a row with a colored circular badge on the left and explanatory text on the right, separated by ample vertical gaps. Further down, a subsidies section reprises the two-column pattern, placing structured subsidy information on the left and visually lighter cards or icons on the right. Near the bottom, a colorful CTA bar spans the content width, gently commanding attention with a cheerful background and large buttons leading to the fee calculator and application pages. When the site is loading or refreshing data, a semi-transparent, warm-tinted overlay with a centered illustrated spinner covers this central column, while a small scroll-to-top button floats unobtrusively at the bottom-right edge of the viewport, independent of the main content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader appears as a semi-transparent overlay that covers the entire central content column—from just below the global site header to just above the page footer. It aligns with the fixed-width container, spanning 100% of its width (roughly 10–11 out of 12 columns) and extending vertically to cover all visible sections: status display, overview, navigation, examples, subsidies, and CTA bar. The overlay uses a warm, slightly tinted background (e.g., soft cream with 60–70% opacity) to dim the underlying content while still letting the shapes of the sections subtly show through, preserving the sense of structure. In the exact center of the viewport, a small, rounded card (about 280–320px wide) floats with generous 24–32px padding, containing a playful illustrated spinner or looping animation and a short loading message set in large, readable type. Around this central card, there is ample empty space to keep the feeling light and uncluttered. On longer pages, the overlay scrolls with the content, always keeping the loader card centered within the current viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay that sits above all other page components within the main content container when active; positioned fixed relative to the viewport so the loader card remains centered while the underlying page cannot be interacted with.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation sits directly below the overview section, centered within the fixed-width content container. It spans about 70–80% of the container width (roughly 8–9 out of 12 columns), leaving soft margins on both sides, and is positioned with 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the bottom edge of the overview panel. The navigation itself is a low-profile, pill-shaped bar with rounded corners, about 48–56px tall, containing 3–4 tabs (e.g., Overview, Fee Examples, Subsidies, Calculator). The tabs are arranged horizontally; the active tab is slightly larger, with a warm, filled background and perhaps a subtle drop shadow, while inactive tabs use soft outlines and muted text colors. The entire navigation bar feels like a friendly control strip that echoes the hero slider’s controls from the mockup: visually aligned towards the right half of the container, with the rightmost tab roughly in line with the right edge of the underlying illustration, encouraging the eye to travel across. There is clear whitespace above and below (16–24px) so it doesn’t crowd adjacent sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Becomes sticky after the user scrolls past the overview section, pinning itself just below the global site header. While sticky, it maintains its width and horizontal centering, providing persistent access to section jumps without obscuring content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fees overview section is the first major section in the content area and visually acts as the page’s hero. It is contained within a large, rounded rectangle panel that spans the full width of the centered content container (about 10–11 of 12 columns) with generous 24–32px padding on all sides and corner radii that echo the mockup’s hero slider. Within this panel, a two-column layout mirrors the ‘Üdvözlet’ section: the left column occupies roughly 55–60% of the width (about 6–7 grid columns), and the right column takes the remaining 40–45% (about 4–5 columns). The left column starts near the top with a large, friendly page title like “Fees & Subsidies Overview,” followed by 16–24px of vertical space before a bold introductory paragraph that summarizes how fees work. Below this intro, there is a vertical stack of 2–3 key points laid out in a mini two-column pattern: a very narrow sub-column on the far left (around 15–20% of the text column) holds small, warm-colored circular badges with numbers or icons; the main sub-column on the right contains the explanatory text for each point. Each bullet row has 16–24px vertical spacing beneath it, preserving the airy feel. The right column houses a large watercolor-style illustration—children near a house, hearts, or a piggy bank motif—centered vertically within the panel and scaled to fill most of the column’s width while leaving comfortable white space around it. The overall height of the section is such that on a typical desktop viewport, it occupies roughly 60–70% of the initial screen height, establishing a welcoming, editorial opening.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section positioned at the top of the main content flow, immediately below any global page header and the status display; adapts responsively by stacking columns vertically on small screens with the illustration moving below the text.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fee examples section sits below the section navigation, separated from it by approximately 48–56px of vertical space to clearly indicate a new block of content. It spans the same fixed-width content container as the overview, but uses a slightly tinted background or soft top and bottom border lines to visually distinguish it from adjacent sections without feeling boxed-in. Inside, the content follows a single primary column for text (around 80–90% of the container width), centered, but each example item is built as a mini two-column row: on the far left, a narrow column (about 10–15% of the text width) holds a circular badge with a number or icon, echoing the numbered circles in the mockup’s welcome bullets; to the right, a much wider text area (85–90% of the text width) contains the fee example title in a slightly larger, bold type, followed by one or two short lines of explanatory text. Each example row is separated vertically by 24–32px, with an additional 16–24px of padding above the first item and below the last to keep the section from feeling crowded. A concise section heading such as “Typical Fee Examples” is aligned to the left at the top of the section, with about 16–24px of space before the first example row. The density remains low: there are no dense tables; instead, 3–5 example rows are laid out with comfortable whitespace so that each can be read at a glance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page; internally, the example rows form a vertical list that can grow or shrink depending on data, maintaining consistent spacing between items.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subsidies section is positioned below the fee examples section, separated by a vertical gap of around 56–64px to maintain the rhythm of major content blocks. It returns to a two-column layout that visually echoes the overview section, reinforcing continuity. Within the centered container, the left column occupies about 55–60% of the width and is devoted to explanatory text: a prominent heading like “Subsidies & Reductions,” followed by 16–24px of space, then 2–4 short paragraphs or bullet groups describing eligibility, how subsidies affect fees, and key conditions. These text blocks have 16–24px gaps between them, keeping the content scannable. The right column (40–45% of the width) contains structured visual aids—such as 2–3 stacked cards or badges—each in its own small rounded rectangle with 16–20px of internal padding. These cards might highlight “Full subsidy,” “Partial subsidy,” and “Sibling discount,” using playful icons and simple numbers, arranged from top to bottom with 16–24px spacing between them. The right column feels visually lighter than the left, with more whitespace and slightly smaller text, acting as a secondary focal area that supports the main explanations. Overall height is moderate; the section typically occupies roughly half a viewport on desktop.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the main scroll flow; maintains its two-column layout on medium and large screens but stacks into a vertical sequence (text first, then visual cards) on small screens while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The CTA bar sits near the bottom of the content stack, immediately following the subsidies section with a vertical gap of about 48–56px. It spans the full width of the centered content container but uses a bright, warm background color (such as a soft golden yellow or coral) and large rounded corners at the top and bottom to distinguish it as a clear, inviting action area. The bar’s height is modest but substantial—around 80–120px depending on content—and it has 24–32px internal padding. Inside, the layout is horizontally split: approximately the left two-thirds hold a short, strong message like “Calculate your exact monthly fee and apply in a few minutes,” set in larger type and aligned to the left; the right one-third contains one or two large primary buttons (e.g., “Open Fee Calculator,” “Start Application”) stacked or aligned side by side, with ample spacing around them (8–12px padding inside each button and 16px between buttons). The buttons nudge slightly towards the right edge of the container, visually echoing the bottom-right focal point from the mockup’s slider arrows and scroll-to-top anchor. There is generous whitespace above and below the text and buttons so the bar feels light, not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow by default, positioned above the page footer. Optionally, on larger screens it can become sticky once it first scrolls into view, gently pinning itself to the bottom of the viewport while leaving room for the floating scroll-to-top button in the lower-right corner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status display is a slim, unobtrusive strip that sits at the very top of the main content area, directly beneath the global page header and above the overview section. It spans roughly 60–80% of the content container width (about 7–9 columns), centered horizontally, and has a gentle pill or rounded-rectangle shape with subtle background tint (for example, a pale yellow or light mint) that differentiates it from the page background without shouting. The height is relatively small—about 40–56px—with 12–16px of vertical padding inside. On the left side of the strip, a small icon (such as an info symbol or calendar) sits with 8–12px of spacing to its right; the status text follows, written in concise, readable language such as “Fee information updated for the 2025 school year” or “You are viewing fees for your selected district.” The text is left-aligned within the strip, but the entire component is visually centered in the page, with 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the overview section below. It is designed to be readable at a glance without competing with the large overview heading that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the top of the content column; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page and does not stick to the viewport.

name: Fee Calculator
title: Fee Calculator
description: Estimate monthly nursery fees based on household income, child age, and schedule type, compare schedule options, and save preferred fee plans for later use on this page.

core_features: Input form that collects householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType, optionally pre-filled from incoming URL parameters householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType.
Fee estimation logic that calls calculateFeeEstimate with the current input values and displays the estimatedMonthlyFee and currency for the selected schedule.

Schedule comparison flow that lets the user switch between schedule types (for example, half_day_5_days, full_day_5_days, other) while keeping income and age, and performs multiple calculateFeeEstimate calls to compare the resulting estimatedMonthlyFee values.
Comparison display that shows the most recent fee estimates for up to two selected schedule types side by side and indicates which schedule currently has the lower estimatedMonthlyFee.

Plan saving functionality that lets the user assign a name to the currently selected schedule and its fee estimate, calls saveFeePlan to persist it as a FeePlan, and then shows it in a saved plans list.

Saved fee plans section that loads the user's existing FeePlan records using getSavedFeePlans and displays basic details (plan name, scheduleType, childAgeYears, householdMonthlyIncome, estimatedMonthlyFee, createdAt) for reference.
Navigation controls to go back to the Fees & Subsidies overview (fees.html) and to start an application (application.html) after a preferred plan is saved.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Open page with optional pre-filled values:
1) User navigates from fees.html to fee_calculator.html, optionally with URL parameters householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType.
2) On page load, the calculator reads these URL parameters.
3) The input form fields for income, child age, and schedule are initialized from these values when present; otherwise, they remain empty or unselected.
4) In parallel, the page calls getSavedFeePlans to retrieve any previously saved FeePlan records for this user and populates the saved plans list.
5) The user can immediately click "Calculate fee" (if inputs are valid) or adjust the inputs before calculating.

Workflow 2 – Calculate fee and compare schedule options:
1) User enters or updates householdMonthlyIncome and childAgeYears in the input form.
2) User selects a scheduleType (e.g., half_day_5_days) as the primary schedule and clicks the "Calculate fee" control.
3) The form validates required fields and triggers calculateFeeEstimate with the current householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and selected scheduleType.
4) When calculateFeeEstimate resolves, the page shows the estimatedMonthlyFee and currency for that schedule and marks it as the first comparison entry.
5) User then changes scheduleType (e.g., to full_day_5_days) while keeping income and age untouched and clicks "Calculate fee" again.
6) A second call to calculateFeeEstimate is made; on success, the new result is stored as another comparison entry.
7) The comparison display shows both entries and programmatically determines which estimatedMonthlyFee is lower, visually indicating that schedule as cheaper.
8) The user can repeat steps 5–7 any number of times, with the comparison display always reflecting the two most recent distinct scheduleType calculations (or more, depending on implementation) and updating the cheaper indicator.

Workflow 3 – Save preferred fee plan and continue to application:
1) After comparing schedules, the user decides on a preferred schedule (either the cheaper one or any selected scheduleType currently having a valid fee estimate).
2) The user opens the "Save plan" interaction (for example, via a button linked to the current calculation) and is prompted to enter a plan name.
3) Upon confirming, the page calls saveFeePlan with name, householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, scheduleType of the chosen schedule, and its estimatedMonthlyFee.
4) When saveFeePlan returns successfully, the new FeePlan is appended to the saved plans list in the UI, and a confirmation state is shown.
5) Optionally, a "Start application" control becomes available or highlighted; when the user activates it, they navigate to application.html (without relying on localStorage), where the application flow continues.
6) At any later time, when the user revisits fee_calculator.html, getSavedFeePlans is called again so they can see the previously saved plans and, if desired, re-enter the same inputs manually based on the saved data.
interactions: User updates input values (income and child age): onChange events update the in-memory state of householdMonthlyIncome and childAgeYears; optional onBlur or onChange handlers synchronize these values to URL parameters householdMonthlyIncome and childAgeYears using the browser history API (e.g., updating the query string for bookmarking and back/forward navigation).

User selects schedule type: onChange of the scheduleType selector updates the in-memory selected schedule and optionally the scheduleType URL parameter; this does not immediately call calculateFeeEstimate until the user explicitly triggers the calculation.

User clicks "Calculate fee": onClick of the calculation control validates that householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType are present and triggers a calculateFeeEstimate call; while the call is in progress, the component shows a loading state for this schedule; onSuccess binds the returned estimatedMonthlyFee, currency, and inputsEcho to the result panel; onError displays an error state and allows retry.

User performs schedule comparison: each time the user changes scheduleType and clicks "Calculate fee", the new result is stored in a comparison data structure (e.g., an array of recent results keyed by scheduleType); an internal comparison function runs after each successful calculateFeeEstimate call to determine which of the currently displayed results has the lower estimatedMonthlyFee and sets a "cheaper" flag on the corresponding entry for rendering.

User opens "Save plan" for current result: onClick of a "Save this plan" control associated with a valid calculation pre-fills a save form with the current householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, scheduleType, and estimatedMonthlyFee; the user only needs to provide a name; cancelling simply closes the form and leaves state unchanged.

User submits "Save plan" form: onSubmit validates that a plan name is present and that there is an associated calculation result; on success, it calls saveFeePlan with name, householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, scheduleType, and estimatedMonthlyFee from the current calculation; onSuccess it receives a FeePlan object, which is appended to the saved plans list; onError it shows a non-blocking error message and keeps the form open for retry or cancel.

User views saved plans: on page load or refresh, an onInit handler calls getSavedFeePlans; when data is returned, a list component renders each FeePlan; selecting a saved plan can update the input form values (income, age, schedule) but does not automatically recalculate the fee until the user explicitly clicks "Calculate fee" again, ensuring the calculation uses the current calculateFeeEstimate logic.

User navigates back to Fees & Subsidies: a "Back to Fees & Subsidies" link or button triggers a navigation to fees.html; it does not depend on localStorage; the user can also use the browser back button, which relies on the URL parameter state already maintained.

User navigates to application: after at least one plan is saved, a "Start application" control is enabled; onClick it navigates to application.html; the fee calculator does not itself write application data but leaves it to the application.html page to ask for necessary details.
page_functionality.state_logic: The main persistent state for this page is derived from and synchronized with URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page reads the query parameters householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType from the URL. If householdMonthlyIncome is present and parseable as a number, it initializes the income input field and the internal income state. If childAgeYears is present and parseable, it initializes the child age field and state. If scheduleType is present and matches an allowed scheduleType value, it initializes the schedule selector. These initial values are purely inputs; no calculation occurs until the user explicitly triggers it. As the user edits the input form, an internal state object mirrors the current householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and selected scheduleType. Optionally, after each valid change, the page updates the URL's query string (using history.replaceState or a similar mechanism) so that reloading the page or using bookmarks preserves the latest input values without using localStorage. Calculation results are kept in in-memory state only (e.g., a collection of recent results keyed by scheduleType); they are not encoded into URL parameters to avoid overly long URLs. Saved plans are not stored in localStorage; instead, they are persisted through saveFeePlan and reloaded via getSavedFeePlans whenever the page is opened or refreshed. The page does not rely on any static or hardcoded numeric defaults; when URL parameters are absent, fields start empty and only gain values from user input or from user-selected saved plans.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, and overall state management for the fee calculator page. On mount, it reads householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType from the URL, initializes shared state, triggers getSavedFeePlans, and passes data and callbacks to child components.

Collects user inputs householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and the currently selected scheduleType, applies basic validation, synchronizes these values with the page state and URL parameters, and triggers calculateFeeEstimate when the user requests a fee calculation.

Encapsulates all interactions with calculateFeeEstimate. Receives validated inputs from the fee-input-form, calls calculateFeeEstimate with householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, and scheduleType, exposes loading and error states, and returns the result object (estimatedMonthlyFee, currency, inputsEcho) to be stored in comparison state.

Maintains and displays the most recent fee calculation results for different scheduleType values. Each time a new calculateFeeEstimate result arrives, it either adds or updates an entry identified by scheduleType. It compares the estimatedMonthlyFee values of the currently displayed entries to determine which schedule is cheaper and sets a flag or computed property used to visually highlight that option.

Receives the current collection of fee calculation results (at least one, optionally more) and computes which scheduleType has the lowest estimatedMonthlyFee among the displayed entries. It exposes this derived information back to the comparison panel or to the user as a clear indication of which schedule is currently cheaper.

Allows the user to save the currently selected calculation as a named FeePlan. It reads the latest successful calculation result (householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, scheduleType, estimatedMonthlyFee) from the shared state, prompts the user only for a name, and then calls saveFeePlan to persist the plan. On success, it notifies the page-shell to update the saved plans list.

Wraps the saveFeePlan interface. It receives the plan name and the calculation context (householdMonthlyIncome, childAgeYears, scheduleType, estimatedMonthlyFee) from the save-plan-form, calls saveFeePlan, and returns the created FeePlan or an error state to the calling component.

Displays all FeePlan records for the user retrieved via getSavedFeePlans. Each list item shows the plan name and key attributes (for example, scheduleType, childAgeYears, householdMonthlyIncome, estimatedMonthlyFee, createdAt). Selecting a plan copies its inputs (income, age, scheduleType) back into the fee-input-form so the user can re-calculate with the current rules.

Handles retrieval of the user's saved fee plans via getSavedFeePlans. It is invoked when the page loads or when a new plan has been successfully saved, and passes the resulting array of FeePlan objects to the saved-plans-list component.

Provides functional navigation actions: a control to return to fees.html and, after at least one plan is saved, a control to proceed to application.html. It does not store navigation-related state in localStorage; navigation is handled by direct URL changes or link clicks.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow with a centered fixed‑width content column on a soft, fluid background. At the very top inside the shell sits the navigation bar. Below that, the first major section is a hero‑style calculator card that visually replaces the mockup's hero slider: it contains the main fee input form on the left and a live comparison/results panel on the right. Immediately underneath, a second full‑width section reuses the mockup's two‑column pattern for saved plans: the saved plans list on the left and the save‑plan form on the right. All visible components are stacked in this order, with generous white space between the two main sections.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top‑to‑bottom flow with a centered fixed‑width content column on a soft, fluid background. At the very top inside the shell sits the navigation bar. Below that, the first major section is a hero‑style calculator card that visually replaces the mockup's hero slider: it contains the main fee input form on the left and a live comparison/results panel on the right. Immediately underneath, a second full‑width section reuses the mockup's two‑column pattern for saved plans: the saved plans list on the left and the save‑plan form on the right. All visible components are stacked in this order, with generous white space between the two main sections.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The page shell spans the full viewport width with a central content area of roughly 1040–1200px using a 12‑column grid. The hero calculator section occupies the full content width and visually takes up about the top half of the scrollable area on a laptop screen. Inside this hero card, roughly 60% of the horizontal space is given to the fee input form (left 7 columns) and 40% to the comparison panel (right 5 columns), mirroring the mockup’s welcome section. Below, the saved‑plans section follows the same 60/40 split: the saved plans list gets more width on the left, while the save‑plan form sits on the right. Vertical gaps between major sections are large (about 48–64px), and each card has 24–32px padding on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains comfortably sparse. Each form field, list item, and comparison row has 16–24px vertical spacing, and text uses larger, friendly type with ample line height to keep the fee calculator from feeling technical or cramped. Major sections sit inside rounded, watercolor‑tinted cards with plenty of breathing room around headings and controls, reflecting the low‑density, editorial feel from the mockup rather than a data‑heavy dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a loose Z‑pattern: the eye starts at the navigation and the large hero heading at the top‑left, then moves across the hero card to the right, where key summary information and the comparison panel live. From there, the gaze drops down along the left edge to the saved plans list, then sweeps across to the right towards the save‑plan form. Within the hero card, the left column (fee inputs) is the primary starting point, and the right column (comparison with cheapest indicator badge) acts as a colorful secondary focal point, echoing the text‑plus‑illustration relationship of the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Fee Calculator page sits on a soft, warm background with a centered content column framed by generous side gutters. At the very top, a rounded, slightly elevated navigation bar spans the width of the content, anchoring the site. Below it, a large hero‑style card fills most of the visible viewport width: its corners are gently rounded, with watercolor‑like tints and playful accent graphics that give it the same friendly, municipal‑nursery feel as the mockup. Inside this card, the left two‑thirds are dedicated to the fee input experience, starting with a bold, chunky display title like “Fee Calculator” and a short, reassuring introductory line, followed by well‑spaced form controls. The right third of the hero card shows the live fee comparison: a compact, carded panel with schedule options and a standout badge in its top‑right corner marking the cheapest schedule option, acting as the visual counterpart to the mockup’s illustration. Beneath this hero card, separated by a generous band of whitespace, a second rounded card presents saved plans management in another two‑column layout: on the left a column of saved plan tiles with clear labels and comfortable spacing, on the right a smaller, cosy form to name and save new plans. Together, these sections create a calm, open, child‑friendly composition, with the main interaction always starting on the left and resolving to a colorful, helpful display on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell extends across the full browser width with a soft, warm background color or subtle watercolor texture. In its center, it defines a fixed‑width content container (approximately 1040–1200px wide) aligned to a 12‑column grid. All visible page content (navigation bar, hero calculator section, saved plans section) is vertically stacked inside this central container. There is generous top padding (about 64px) above the hero calculator card and similar bottom padding (48–64px) below the last section to prevent content from feeling cramped against the viewport edges. The shell ensures 24–32px horizontal padding on smaller screens so that cards never touch the browser edges, maintaining the soft, rounded, municipal nursery feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the primary structural container in normal document flow, centering content and constraining maximum width while allowing the background to be full‑bleed and fluid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls form a horizontal bar pinned to the very top of the content column, spanning 100% of the page shell’s central width. It has a slightly rounded rectangular shape and a subtle shadow or darker watercolor band to distinguish it from the hero card below while still feeling soft and friendly. Vertically, it is relatively compact (roughly 64–80px tall) with 16–24px padding above and below the navigation items. The logo or site title sits toward the left, with main navigation links flowing to the right, following the same playful yet legible typography as the rest of the site. There is a comfortable gap of about 32px between the bottom of the navigation bar and the top of the hero calculator card to echo the airy spacing from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky to the top of the viewport when the user scrolls, keeping navigation always visible while the rest of the page scrolls beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fee input form sits inside the first major hero‑style card, anchored to the left side and occupying roughly 55–60% of that card’s width (about 7 of 12 grid columns). The hero card itself is centered within the page shell and stretched nearly edge‑to‑edge within the content width, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides. At the top of the left column, a large, friendly heading (e.g., “Fee Calculator”) is placed flush left, with about 16–24px spacing below it before a brief explanatory sentence. Below this text, the form fields are arranged in a clean vertical stack with 16–24px gaps between groups (e.g., household income, child age, schedule type). Each input has generous horizontal padding and rounded corners to stay in line with the playful, child‑friendly aesthetic. The entire form column reaches from near the top of the hero card down to about two‑thirds of its height, leaving some breathable spacing at the bottom so the card border remains visible.
