There is a clear right margin (24–32px) between the form column and the comparison panel on the right to reinforce the two‑column structure from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero calculator card, occupying the left columns on wide screens. On smaller screens, it stacks above the fee-comparison-panel with full width and preserved vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fee calculation service connector is not visible as a UI element. Logically, it is associated with the fee input form and the comparison panel, so it is treated as residing directly behind these components. It does not create any box, card, or occupied area on the page; instead, it simply powers the data exchange between the inputs and the displayed fee results, updating values whenever the user changes form fields.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and does not occupy physical space. It behaves like an invisible layer tied to the fee-input-form and fee-comparison-panel, triggered by interactions but not influencing layout or flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fee comparison panel is placed within the same hero card as the fee input form, anchored to the right edge and occupying roughly 40–45% of the card’s width (about 5 of 12 columns). It aligns vertically with the form’s top edge: its header area (e.g. “Your Monthly Estimate”) is horizontally aligned with the form’s first field or introduction text, reinforcing the left-to-right reading order. The panel is styled as a contained card within the larger hero card, with a slightly lighter or contrasting watercolor tint and 16–24px internal padding. Inside, schedule options are arranged in a vertically stacked list of rows or mini-cards, each with comfortable spacing, clear labels (e.g. “Half-day,” “Full-day”), and the calculated fee value. The panel height generally matches or slightly exceeds the height of the left column’s form content, creating a balanced, rectangular two-column block. There is a narrow white margin between this panel and the hero card’s right edge (16–24px), echoing the margin between the form and the panel. The cheapest-schedule-indicator is visually attached to the top-right of this panel, acting as a focal accent similar to an illustration in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero calculator card, anchored to the right columns on wide screens. On smaller screens, it moves below the fee-input-form, taking full width while preserving its internal padding and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cheapest schedule indicator appears as a compact, visually prominent badge associated with the fee comparison panel. It is positioned overlaying the upper right corner of the fee-comparison-panel, slightly offset inward (about 8–12px from the top and right edges of the panel’s inner padding), mimicking a sticker or ribbon. The badge uses a bright, warm highlight color (such as a saturated yellow or coral) with rounded edges and playful typography, making it stand out like the yellow numbered circles in the mockup. Its size is modest—roughly the width of a short label and 32–40px tall—so it does not overpower the panel but clearly signals the most affordable option. Internally, it might display text like “Cheapest option” or a simple icon-plus-label, centered both horizontally and vertically. The indicator’s position and color draw the eye in a similar way the illustration did in the original design, acting as a visual anchor on the right side of the hero section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an overlay, absolutely anchored relative to the fee-comparison-panel container. It moves with the panel when layout responds, staying pinned to its top-right corner both on wide and narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save plan form lives in the second major section below the hero card, positioned on the right side of a full-width, rounded rectangle card that mirrors the styling of the hero section. Inside this saved plans card, the layout again uses a two-column structure. The save plan form occupies the right 40–45% of the card (about 5 of 12 grid columns), aligning vertically with the top of the saved plans list on the left. At the top of this right column is a medium-sized heading such as “Save this plan,” followed by a short line of helper text explaining what will be saved. Below, a few form fields (plan name, optional notes, maybe a color/icon selector) are arranged in a vertical stack with 16–20px spacing. The form ends with a rounded primary button that has 16–20px top margin, echoing the chunky, friendly button style from the mockup. There is at least 24px of left padding separating the form column from the saved plans list, and 24–32px inner padding from the right card edge. The overall column is shorter and lighter than the hero form, giving it a clear but secondary role.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the saved-plans section card, anchored to the right columns on large screens. On smaller screens, it stacks below the saved-plans-list, taking full width with the same generous padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save plan service connector is non-visual and conceptually attached to the save plan form and saved plans list. It does not appear as a box, button, or text; instead, it silently handles sending the currently calculated fee data and form inputs to the backend when the user saves a plan. It has no effect on the overall alignment, spacing, or visual rhythm of the saved plans section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial, functioning purely as background logic associated with submissions from the save-plan-form and updates to the saved-plans-list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved plans list is the primary element of the second major section and is placed on the left side of its rounded card, directly mirroring the left-column emphasis seen in the mockup’s welcome section. It occupies about 55–60% of the card’s width (around 7 of 12 grid columns) and aligns flush left with the card’s internal padding (24–32px). At the top of the list column, a clear heading like “Saved Plans” appears with a slightly bold, friendly face, followed by a short explanatory line. Below this, individual saved plans are displayed as vertically stacked, softly rounded mini-cards or rows with 16–24px spacing between them. Each item has enough breathing room to show the plan name, key parameters (schedule type, child age, fee), and simple action controls (e.g., “Load,” “Delete”) arranged horizontally inside the card. The list area extends downward, taking up most of the vertical space in this section. If the list grows long, it continues in the page’s natural scroll rather than creating an internal scrollbar, aligning with the editorial, open feel of the design. The right-column save-plan-form runs parallel to the top portion of this list, but the list may extend further down, reinforcing its importance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the left columns of the saved-plans section, participating fully in the normal document flow. On smaller screens, it appears above the save-plan-form, taking full width and pushing the form downward as the list grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved plans service connector is entirely invisible and associated with the saved plans list’s data state. It is responsible for loading existing saved plans when the page is opened and updating the list when plans are added or removed. It has no on-screen representation and does not alter the spacing or alignment of the saved-plans-list component or its parent section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual background component with no footprint; functions behind the saved-plans-list, reacting to page load and save/delete actions without affecting layout.

Static within the hero calculator card, anchored to the right columns on wide screens. On smaller screens, it moves below the fee-input-form, taking full width while preserving its internal padding and spacing.

name: Application Wizard
title: Nursery Application
description: Multi-step wizard for creating and editing a municipal nursery application, including child and parent details, desired schedule, and ranked nursery choices.

core_features: Load an existing draft or submitted application (and any pre-selected nursery preferences) using the applicationId URL parameter via getApplicationState.
Display and edit core application details (desired start date, care type, schedule type, child details, parent contact details, and district filter) and save changes via updateApplicationDetails.
Show the current list of nurseries associated with the application, including their preference order, using data from getApplicationState and setApplicationNurseryPreferences.
Provide an embedded nursery search and filter section that obtains dynamic filter options via getNurserySearchFilterOptions and nursery results via searchNurseries.
Allow the user to add searched nurseries to the application via addNurseryToApplication and remove them via removeNurseryFromApplication.
Enable reordering of nursery preferences (e.g., drag-and-drop or order selectors) and persist the ranked order via setApplicationNurseryPreferences.

Support step-based navigation (e.g., details → nursery selection → review & submit), persisting data at each step via updateApplicationDetails and setApplicationNurseryPreferences before moving to the next step.
Submit the completed application via submitApplication and show a submission confirmation with an option to navigate back to the home page (index.html).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Continue a draft application with pre-selected nurseries
1. User navigates to application.html with applicationId in the URL from nursery_detail, nurseries, favorites, calendar, or fee_calculator.
2. On page load, the wizard reads applicationId from the URL and calls getApplicationState.
3. The wizard displays the existing application details (if any) and pre-selected nursery choices in the designated steps.
4. In the "Application details" step, the user edits desired start date, care type, schedule, child information, and parent contact details. On clicking "Save and continue", the wizard calls updateApplicationDetails and then updates the URL step parameter to the next step.
5. In the "Nursery choices" step, the user sees the current preference list (from getApplicationState) and the embedded search. The user optionally adjusts the district filter and other filters, then triggers searchNurseries. From the results, the user adds nurseries via addNurseryToApplication, removes any via removeNurseryFromApplication, and reorders them. On save, the wizard calls setApplicationNurseryPreferences with the new order and updates the URL step parameter.
6. In the "Review & submit" step, the user reviews all details and preferences. If required, the user can navigate back to previous steps (changing the step parameter) to correct data. When satisfied, the user clicks "Submit application", which calls submitApplication.
7. After a successful response, the wizard shows the returned application status and message, and exposes a "Back to home" action that navigates to index.html.

Workflow 2: Adjust nursery preferences only, keeping details unchanged
1. User opens application.html with applicationId and a URL step indicating the nursery-selection step.
2. On load, the wizard calls getApplicationState and positions the UI on the specified step.
3. The nursery preference list is shown, including pre-selected nurseries and their current order.
4. The user uses embedded search (searchNurseries) to find additional nurseries and adds them via addNurseryToApplication.
5. The user reorders the list by adjusting preference positions and confirms the new order, prompting setApplicationNurseryPreferences.
6. The user proceeds to the review step and, if ready, submits via submitApplication or leaves the application as a draft by leaving the page without submission; the saved preferences remain accessible in future via getApplicationState.

Workflow 3: Return to a submitted application for read-only review
1. User opens application.html with applicationId for an already submitted application.
2. On load, the wizard calls getApplicationState and detects that application.status is submitted.
3. All steps render in a read-only mode (fields disabled, no add/remove/reorder actions). No calls to updateApplicationDetails, addNurseryToApplication, removeNurseryFromApplication, or setApplicationNurseryPreferences are made from user actions.
4. The user reviews the details and uses a provided navigation action to go back to index.html or another page; submitApplication is not exposed in this state.
interactions:
Initial load
- onInit: When the page loads, read applicationId and optional step from the URL.
- If applicationId is present: call getApplicationState(applicationId) and populate the wizard state (application details and nursery preferences).
- If applicationId is missing or getApplicationState fails: show an error state and disable all step interactions (no API calls beyond the initial attempt).

Step navigation
- onStepChange (e.g., clicking Next/Previous step buttons or a step indicator):
  - Validate the current step’s required fields at UI level.
  - If moving forward from the details step: call updateApplicationDetails with the current form values before updating the URL step parameter.
  - If moving forward from the nursery step: call setApplicationNurseryPreferences with the current ordered list before updating the URL step parameter.
  - On moving backward: no write calls are required; the wizard simply updates the URL step parameter and re-renders existing data from local in-memory state (originating from getApplicationState and any prior updates).

Application details form interactions
- onDesiredStartDateChange, onCareTypeChange, onScheduleTypeChange, onChildFieldChange, onParentFieldChange, onDistrictFilterChange: update local form state only.
- onDetailsSaveOrNext (e.g., "Save" or "Save and continue"):
  - Prepare parameters for updateApplicationDetails using the current form state.
  - Call updateApplicationDetails(applicationId, desiredStartDate, careType, scheduleType, childName, childDateOfBirth, parentName, parentPhone, parentEmail, districtFilter).
  - On success, update the in-memory application object and optionally move to the next step (updating the URL step parameter).

Embedded nursery search filter interactions
- onFiltersInit: When the nursery step is first entered, call getNurserySearchFilterOptions to populate filter dropdowns (districts, careTypes, ratingOptions, etc.).
- onFilterChange (e.g., district, care type, distance, opening time, rating, fee range, dietary options, sortBy): update local filter state.
- onSearchSubmit (e.g., clicking a "Search" or "Update results" button within the embedded search):
  - Compute childAgeYears if both childDateOfBirth and a relevant reference date (such as desiredStartDate or current date) are available from the application; otherwise, pass an undefined or empty value for childAgeYears to searchNurseries.
  - Call searchNurseries with the current filter state, using district and careType from either the filter controls or the existing application.districtFilter and application.careType where appropriate.
  - Display the returned list of nurseries within the embedded search results component.

Nursery selection interactions
- onAddNurseryClick (from a search result item):
  - Call addNurseryToApplication(applicationId, nurseryId) for the clicked nursery.
  - On success, append the returned preference and nursery to the in-memory preference list, preserving the preferenceOrder returned by the interface.
- onRemoveNurseryClick (from the current preference list):
  - Call removeNurseryFromApplication(applicationId, nurseryId).
  - On success, refresh the in-memory preference list by either:
    - Updating the preference orders locally (based on known rules such as reindexing), or
    - Re-fetching the current application state via getApplicationState if needed.
- onPreferenceReorder (e.g., drag-and-drop or changing numeric order fields):
  - Update the local list of preferences to reflect the new order.
  - On explicit save (e.g., clicking a "Save order" button) or when moving to the next step, call setApplicationNurseryPreferences(applicationId, preferences) with the final ordered list.

Review & submit interactions
- onReviewInit: When entering the review step, ensure that the local application state and nursery preferences reflect the latest data (from getApplicationState and any updates made in prior steps).
- onSubmitApplicationClick:
  - Call submitApplication(applicationId).
  - On success, display the returned application object and message, and disable further editing across all steps.
- onBackToHomeClick (from confirmation state): navigate to index.html without additional API calls.

URL parameter interactions
- applicationId (required for functional use): controls which application is loaded and updated in all API calls.
- step (optional, numeric or string): determines which wizard step is initially active. Step changes triggered by the UI update this parameter using standard navigation methods so that reloading the page retains the current step.
- The page does not use localStorage for persistence; all persisted state is maintained on the server via the assigned interfaces and reloaded via getApplicationState using applicationId from the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary persistent identifier for this page is the applicationId URL parameter. On every load, the page reads applicationId and calls getApplicationState(applicationId) to retrieve the canonical application and nursery preference state. The wizard’s internal step is controlled via a step URL parameter (for example, application.html?applicationId=...&step=details), which the UI updates whenever the user moves between steps. This means that a page refresh preserves both the application in context and the current step without relying on localStorage.

During a session, the wizard maintains in-memory state for form fields, filter values, search results, and preference ordering, all derived from the results of getApplicationState, getNurserySearchFilterOptions, and searchNurseries, plus user input. However, only calls to updateApplicationDetails, addNurseryToApplication, removeNurseryFromApplication, setApplicationNurseryPreferences, and submitApplication change the server-side state. After such calls, the wizard either updates its in-memory representation from the returned data or, if needed, re-syncs via a follow-up getApplicationState call.

The application.status field (obtained via getApplicationState and returned in updateApplicationDetails and submitApplication responses) controls whether the UI is editable. If status is draft, all editing features are enabled. If status is submitted or cancelled, all editing actions that would call update or mutation interfaces are disabled in the UI, and user interactions become read-only. Navigation to index.html after submission is a standard link or redirect that does not pass additional parameters. No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used; all persisted data flows through the assigned interfaces based solely on applicationId and user-triggered actions.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of application data, step-based navigation, and global state management for the wizard. On initialization, reads applicationId and step from the URL, calls getApplicationState to obtain the Application and associated nurseryPreferences, and then delegates data to child components. It also enforces read-only mode if application.status is not draft.

Displays and edits core application details including desired start date, careType, scheduleType, childName, childDateOfBirth, parentName, parentPhone, parentEmail, and districtFilter. On load, fields are populated from the Application returned by getApplicationState. On save or step transition, it calls updateApplicationDetails with the current field values and updates the wizard’s in-memory Application state from the response.

Shows all nurseries currently attached to the application, with their preferenceOrder values and key information from the Nursery entity. It allows users (when application.status is draft) to remove nurseries from the application and to change their order. Removals call removeNurseryFromApplication; reordering is captured locally and persisted via setApplicationNurseryPreferences when the user saves the changes or proceeds to the next step.

Provides the embedded search/filter UI used to find additional nurseries to add to the application. On first use, it calls getNurserySearchFilterOptions to populate dynamic filter controls such as districts, careTypes, rating thresholds, fee ranges, opening times, dietary options, and sort options. When the user submits the filter form, it computes childAgeYears if possible (from childDateOfBirth and a suitable reference date) and then calls searchNurseries with the current filter values and relevant criteria from the Application (e.g., careType, districtFilter), updating the associated results component with the returned list.

Displays the list of nurseries returned by searchNurseries, along with their key attributes (such as name, districtLabel, distanceFromHomeKm, ratingAverage, baseMonthlyFee, estimatedMonthlyFeeForCriteria, care-type flags, and dietary flags). For each result, it exposes an action to add that nursery to the current application. When the user clicks this action, it calls addNurseryToApplication with the relevant nurseryId, then updates the nursery-preference-list component with the returned preference and nursery.

Highlights the current nursery choices and their preference order at the top or side of the nursery selection step to satisfy the requirement of starting with pre-selected nurseries from previous pages. It is read-only and simply mirrors the latest nursery preference data from getApplicationState and any updates made via addNurseryToApplication, removeNurseryFromApplication, and setApplicationNurseryPreferences.

Aggregates a read-only overview of all application details and nursery preferences for the final step. It presents the Application fields and the ordered list of Nursery preferences so the user can verify them before submission. If the application is still in draft, it exposes a control to submit the application via submitApplication and reacts to the returned Application and message by updating the wizard-shell and transitioning to a confirmation sub-state.

Manages the logical step progression (e.g., details → nurseries → review). It reads the current step from the URL, emits step change events when the user navigates between steps, and updates the URL step parameter to reflect the current step. Before moving forward, it triggers the appropriate save actions on the current step (updateApplicationDetails or setApplicationNurseryPreferences). It also ensures that navigation to editing steps is disabled when application.status is not draft.

Displays after a successful call to submitApplication, showing the returned application.status and message. It confirms that preferences and details have been saved and submitted. It also offers a "Back to home" action that navigates to index.html using a standard navigation event, without performing additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: The wizard inherits the mockup’s structure: a single, centered, rounded rectangle acting as the main wizard shell on a full-width, softly colored background. Inside this shell, each step follows a stacked layout: a compact header/step navigation band at the top (analogous to the hero slider’s header area), and a main content area below that adopts the two-column pattern from the ‘Üdvözlet’ section. The left column consistently holds the primary interactive content (forms, filters, results), while the right column hosts supportive, secondary content (preferences, summaries) or decorative illustration space. Only one major step’s content is visible at a time; components associated with other steps are hidden but maintain the same structural logic when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The wizard inherits the mockup’s structure: a single, centered, rounded rectangle acting as the main wizard shell on a full-width, softly colored background. Inside this shell, each step follows a stacked layout: a compact header/step navigation band at the top (analogous to the hero slider’s header area), and a main content area below that adopts the two-column pattern from the ‘Üdvözlet’ section. The left column consistently holds the primary interactive content (forms, filters, results), while the right column hosts supportive, secondary content (preferences, summaries) or decorative illustration space. Only one major step’s content is visible at a time; components associated with other steps are hidden but maintain the same structural logic when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The wizard shell spans roughly 80–90% of the available page width up to a fixed max-width (around 1040–1200px), centered horizontally with generous margins to the viewport edge. Inside, the main content area uses a stable 60/40 split: about 55–60% of the width for the left content column and 40–45% for the right column, reflecting the mockup’s text/illustration balance. Vertical spacing between the navigation band and main content, and between major content blocks, follows the 48–64px section spacing, with inner content separated by 24–32px. Each component’s internal padding uses 24–32px for panels and 8–12px for small interactive elements like buttons and badges.
layout_strategies.content_density: The wizard maintains a low to moderate content density. Form fields are stacked in a single column within their side of the grid to avoid clutter. Sections of the forms and summaries are grouped with clear headings and 16–24px vertical gaps, creating distinct visual blocks. Lists (results, preferences) have generous line height and 16–24px vertical spacing between items. There is visible whitespace around all panels, especially around the right-hand summary/preference column, which echoes the airy editorial feel and avoids a dashboard-like density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow echoes the Z-pattern: the eye starts at the top-left with the wizard title and active step label, moves across to the top-right where later-step indicators or contextual actions sit, then drops diagonally to the main left column where the primary task (filling forms, filtering, reviewing) happens. From there, attention moves down through vertical content groups, with periodic pulls to the right column where colorful summary/preference panels live, functioning like the illustration in the mockup. A floating scroll-to-top button (global to the site) remains anchored in the bottom-right of the viewport, lightly counterbalancing the composition without competing with the wizard content.

layout.overall_description: The Application Wizard page sits on a soft, warm background, with a large, rounded rectangular wizard shell centered on the screen. This shell feels like a friendly card laid on a desk: generous 32–40px padding on all sides, softly rounded corners, and subtle shadowing. At its top, a horizontal band contains the step navigation and current-step title, mirroring the hero area of the mockup but scaled down for a wizard context. Below, the shell is divided into two columns: a wider left column occupying about 60% of the shell’s width where users carry out their main tasks (completing forms, searching nurseries, reviewing details), and a narrower right column of about 40% for playful but useful side content like ranked nursery choices or summary panels. Vertically, sections breathe; each step’s content fills most of the viewport height with comfortable space between grouped elements. The user’s eye naturally travels from the header’s step navigation, down into the left interaction column, and then across to the right support column, echoing the mockup’s Z-shaped reading path. Throughout, rounded boxes, numbered badges, and soft, illustrative accents reinforce the daycare-friendly, slightly retro tone, while the global scroll-to-top button floats quietly at the bottom-right of the viewport, independent of the shell.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-wizard-shell is the dominant, centered container card on the page. It sits on a full-width, warm-toned background and is horizontally centered with equal left and right margins. The shell spans about 80–90% of the viewport width up to a fixed max-width (roughly 1040–1200px) and has generous 32–40px internal padding on all sides. Its corners are distinctly rounded, echoing the hero slider’s rounded rectangle from the mockup, and it casts a soft, unobtrusive shadow to lift it from the background. Inside the shell, the top 15–20% of its height is reserved for the wizard-step-navigation band, which stretches nearly the full width, leaving 24–32px of padding from the top and sides. Below this band, the remaining vertical space is divided into the familiar two-column layout: a left content column occupying about 60% of the internal width and a right content column taking up about 40%, with a 24–32px gutter between them. All other components for a given step sit within these columns; they never extend beyond the shell’s rounded boundary. Vertically, components inside the shell are stacked with 24–32px gaps, maintaining the airy editorial feeling of the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered container that defines the main content area; scrolls vertically with the page while maintaining its central alignment and rounded, card-like presence.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wizard-step-navigation is a horizontal strip anchored to the top inside edge of the application-wizard-shell. It runs almost the full inner width of the shell, leaving about 24px of horizontal padding on both sides. Vertically, it occupies a band of around 64–80px height. On the left side of this band, taking roughly the left 60% of this width, sits the current step title and a short, bold introductory line—visually akin to a mini hero headline—with large, friendly typography and generous line spacing. To the right, aligned with the band’s vertical center and occupying the remaining 40%, is the stepper: a row of 3–5 step indicators (e.g., numbered circles or icons) spaced evenly along a horizontal line. These indicators mimic the mockup’s numbered badges, using circular shapes with soft, warm colors; the current step’s badge is slightly larger and filled, while future steps are outlined or muted. Steps are connected by a subtle line to imply progression. The stepper aligns visually with where the hero slider’s navigation arrows would appear in the original mockup—towards the upper-right—maintaining the Z-flow from headline to navigation elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears at the top of the shell on all steps; within the shell it can optionally behave as sticky while scrolling long content, remaining pinned to the top inner edge of the shell when the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-details-form is positioned directly beneath the wizard-step-navigation in the left column of the wizard shell during the initial step. It occupies roughly 55–60% of the shell’s internal width, starting about 24–32px below the navigation band. The form is organized as a vertical stack of grouped sections (e.g., Child details, Parent/guardian details, Contact information). Each group begins with a medium-sized, friendly heading aligned flush left, followed by a short descriptive line for clarity; these headings have about 16–24px of space above and below them. Form fields themselves are arranged in a single column to preserve the low-density, editorial feel: each field label and input pair spans the full width of the left column with 16–20px vertical spacing between fields, and 24–32px between field groups. The right column of the shell, opposite the form, remains visually lighter: it may display a decorative illustration or a soft, informational panel aligned to the top-right, occupying about 70–80% of the right column’s width and leaving whitespace around it to mirror the ‘Üdvözlet’ illustration block. This right-hand space visually balances the dense form fields on the left without competing for attention. At the bottom of the form, primary and secondary buttons are aligned to the left edge of the form content, with about 24–32px of top margin separating them from the last form field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column of the shell; it scrolls naturally with the page, with the parent wizard-step-navigation optionally remaining sticky above it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nursery-search-filters component appears in the left column of the wizard shell when the user reaches the nursery selection step. It sits immediately below the wizard-step-navigation, occupying the full width of the left column (about 60% of the shell). This filter area is encapsulated in a slightly raised, rounded panel with 24–32px internal padding, giving it a card-like feel distinct from the background of the shell itself. At the top of the panel, a bold filter headline (e.g., “Search nurseries”) and a one-line helper text mirror the structure of the ‘Üdvözlet’ heading and intro, taking up the top 20–25% of the panel’s vertical space. Below, filter controls (location, age group, schedule, special needs, etc.) are laid out vertically with 16–20px gaps between rows, and 24px between major filter groups. Chips, dropdowns, and toggle buttons are arranged in short horizontal clusters but always constrained within the left column’s width, avoiding a crowded feel. At the bottom-right of the filter panel (still within the left column), a prominent ‘Search’ button is positioned with 16–24px of space above it. The right column, parallel to this filter area, contains the nursery-preselection-summary and nursery-preference-list components, creating a balanced, two-column composition akin to the text-and-illustration pairing in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static panel at the top of the left column for this step; as the user scrolls past long search results below, the filter panel scrolls out of view unless the viewport height allows it to remain partially visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nursery-search-results component is anchored directly beneath the nursery-search-filters in the left column, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the left column and extends downward as far as needed, often consuming most of the vertical space on this step. The results are presented as a vertical list of nursery cards, each card having rounded corners, 24px internal padding, and 16–24px vertical spacing between cards to maintain the airy, child-friendly aesthetic. Each card’s content follows a left-to-right micro Z-flow: nursery name and key details start at the top-left of the card, while a playful selection control (e.g., ‘Add to preferences’ button) appears on the top-right, possibly with a small icon or badge. Secondary information (address, distance, opening hours) flows below in 1–2 lines with generous line height. The entire list aligns visually with the stacked bullet paragraphs from the mockup, but the numbered circles are replaced by selection controls or small status badges where appropriate. As the user scrolls through results, the right column containing the nursery-preference-list and nursery-preselection-summary remains visible (if sticky), allowing the user to see their current selection while exploring options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls vertically within the page as a long list; occupies the lower portion of the left column and can extend beyond the viewport, with no fixed or sticky behavior of its own.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nursery-preference-list resides in the right column of the wizard shell on the nursery selection step, forming the main content of that column. It is positioned below or beside the nursery-preselection-summary, starting near the top of the right column with a 24px gap between them if stacked. The list occupies about 80–90% of the right column’s width, centered within it to leave comfortable side margins, and extends vertically as needed. Its structure echoes the numbered bullets from the ‘Üdvözlet’ section: each nursery in the ranking appears as a horizontal row with a bold nursery name on the left and a playful, circular rank badge on the far left margin or inline at the start of the row. These rank badges are colored, rounded circles containing numbers (1, 2, 3, etc.), visually referencing the yellow numbered circles in the mockup. Items are separated by 16–24px vertical spacing and may include drag handles or up/down icons aligned to the right, enabling reordering without heavy visual clutter. The overall density is low: no more than 3–5 key nurseries are visible at once without scrolling. The list’s top heading (e.g., “Your chosen nurseries”) and a short helper line sit above the items with about 16–24px spacing, mirroring the left column’s section headings.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned within the right column and recommended to be sticky relative to the viewport within the shell; as the user scrolls through a long list of search results in the left column, the preference list remains pinned near the top of the right column, updating in place when items are added or reordered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The nursery-preselection-summary is a compact panel at the top of the right column during the nursery selection step, sitting directly under the wizard-step-navigation’s vertical alignment and just above the nursery-preference-list. It spans roughly 80–90% of the right column’s width and is centered, leaving equal margins on both sides. The panel height is modest—around 96–144px—maintaining a lightweight feel. Inside, content is grouped like a small editorial teaser: a bold title (e.g., “Application status”) on the top-left, a key figure or short phrase (e.g., “3 nurseries selected”) presented prominently beneath, and possibly one or two concise bullet-style lines summarizing constraints or reminders. The bullets can use miniature circular icons or badges echoing the mockup’s numbered circles but without explicit numbers, keeping the panel friendly and at-a-glance. There is 16–24px of internal padding all around and 16px between text rows. The panel’s background color contrasts softly with the shell to make it stand out as a status element without overpowering the main left-column content. This summary panel provides a quick snapshot that draws the eye to the right, balancing the visual weight of the filters and results on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static relative to the right column but typically grouped into the same sticky region as the nursery-preference-list; when the right column sticks, this summary sits directly at the top of that sticky block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The application-review-summary occupies both columns of the wizard shell on the final review step, but still respects the 60/40 distribution to preserve the design language. The component begins about 24–32px below the wizard-step-navigation and stretches across the full width of the shell, then internally splits into a wider left review column and a narrower right recap column. The left review column (about 60% width) presents the detailed breakdown of the application: child details, guardian details, schedule, and selected nurseries. Each section appears as a rounded sub-card with 24px padding, separated from the next section by 24–32px of vertical space. Section headings are bold and playful, with 16–24px padding above and below, and the content below each heading is formatted as short paragraphs or bullet lists with comfortable line height—similar to the numbered paragraphs of the mockup but without the large badges. On the right, occupying about 40% of the width, a vertical stack of compact summary cards gives a high-level overview: one card for key personal info, another for schedule, another for top nursery choice(s). These cards mimic the nursery-preselection-summary style, with concise titles, key numbers or icons, and mini bullet lines, leaving 16–24px between cards. The visual effect is that the left side supports deeper verification, while the right side provides at-a-glance reassurance, maintaining a balanced Z-pattern as users scan back and forth. Action buttons (e.g., ‘Submit application’, ‘Edit step’) are aligned at the bottom-left of the left column, with generous 24–32px spacing from the last summary block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the shell; scrollable vertically if the review content exceeds the viewport height, with the step navigation optionally fixed at the top of the shell for context.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-confirmation-panel replaces the main step content inside the wizard shell once the application has been successfully submitted. It is visually more centralized and hero-like compared to the forms and lists. The panel is centered both horizontally and vertically within the shell’s inner area (with at least 48px padding from the shell edges), spanning roughly 70–80% of the shell width and auto-height based on content. At the top-center of the panel, a large, friendly confirmation icon (e.g., a checkmark inside a soft, colored circle) sits with ample whitespace around it, echoing the illustrative, playful nature of the original mockup. Directly below, a bold, warm headline (e.g., “Application submitted!”) spans about 60% of the panel width and is centered, followed by a short, two- to three-line explanatory paragraph with generous line height and 16–24px spacing below the headline. Under this, a compact, left-aligned information block lists key submission details (reference number, date, next steps) using 2–4 lines with 8–16px spacing, formatted similarly to the numbered content in the ‘Üdvözlet’ section but without badges, to keep the panel calm and celebratory. At the bottom, primary and secondary buttons are centered horizontally with 16–24px space between them and at least 24px of margin above. The surrounding area of the shell remains mostly empty, emphasizing the confirmation as a clear, singular focal point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, centered content within the wizard shell after submission; it scrolls only if the viewport height is too small, but typically appears fully within the initial viewport to emphasize completion.

name: Calendar & Closures
title: Calendar & Closures
description: Shows which days municipal nurseries and daycare centers are open or closed, lets users navigate months, find the first open day after a given date, and start an application from a selected open day.

core_features: Display a monthly calendar using getCalendarMonthDays(year, month) with each day marked as open, closed, or partial and optional notes.
Allow navigation between months and years (e.g., previous/next month, direct month/year selection) and update URL parameters accordingly.
Allow clicking on an open or partial day to select it and show detailed status and notes for that date.
Provide an action button "Apply from this date" that uses startApplicationFromCalendarDate(desiredStartDate) and redirects to application.html with the returned applicationId.
Provide a "Find first open day after" helper that accepts a date, calls getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate), and highlights/navigates to the resulting open day.
Support deep-linking via URL parameters year, month, and fromDate so users can share or bookmark a specific calendar view and first-open-day query.
user_workflows: View a specific month: (1) User opens calendar.html, optionally with year and month in the URL. (2) Page reads year and month from the URL if present; otherwise it determines the current month/year from the system date. (3) Page calls getCalendarMonthDays(year, month). (4) The calendar grid displays the days with their status and notes.

Navigate to another month: (1) User clicks a "Previous month" or "Next month" control, or chooses a specific month/year from a selector. (2) Component computes the new year and month values. (3) URL is updated to calendar.html?year=YYYY&month=M (retaining fromDate if appropriate). (4) Page calls getCalendarMonthDays(newYear, newMonth) and updates the calendar grid.

Select a day and review details: (1) User clicks on a day cell in the calendar grid. (2) If the day comes from getCalendarMonthDays and is not marked as closed, it becomes the selected day in page state. (3) A details panel shows the selected date, its open/closed/partial status, and any note. (4) If the selected day is open (or partial, depending on municipal rules), the "Apply from this date" button becomes enabled.

Start an application from the calendar: (1) User selects an open day as above. (2) User clicks the enabled "Apply from this date" button. (3) Component calls startApplicationFromCalendarDate(desiredStartDate=<selected date in 'YYYY-MM-DD'>). (4) When the call returns an Application object, the component reads application.id. (5) Browser navigates to application.html?applicationId=<id>.

Find first open day after a given date (direct input): (1) User enters a reference date into the "Find first open day after" input (format 'YYYY-MM-DD'). (2) User submits the form. (3) Component updates the URL to include fromDate=<entered date>, e.g., calendar.html?fromDate=YYYY-MM-DD (and sets or keeps year/month if needed). (4) Component calls getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate=<entered date>). (5) If a result date is returned, component updates year and month in the URL to match that date and calls getCalendarMonthDays for that month. (6) The calendar grid scrolls/updates to that month and highlights the returned open day as selected in the UI.

Use fromDate deep-link to see first open day: (1) User opens calendar.html with a fromDate URL parameter, optionally also year and month. (2) Page reads fromDate from the URL. (3) Page calls getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate). (4) If an open date is returned, the page sets year and month to that date’s year and month (overriding any conflicting URL month/year), updates the URL, and calls getCalendarMonthDays for that month. (5) The returned calendarDay is highlighted as selected in the calendar grid and shown in the details panel.

Handle case where no open day is found: (1) User submits a fromDate, or arrives with a fromDate URL parameter. (2) getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate) returns an object where date is an empty string (no open day found). (3) The page keeps the currently selected year/month from the URL or system date, and calls getCalendarMonthDays for that month if not already loaded. (4) The details panel shows that no open day could be found after the requested date and does not show the "Apply from this date" button.
interactions: Month change interaction: Clicking previous/next month or changing month/year in a selector triggers a state update and URL rewrite with year and month, followed by a call to getCalendarMonthDays(year, month).

Day selection interaction: Clicking a calendar cell for a date that exists in the getCalendarMonthDays result sets that CalendarDay as the selectedDay in page state, updates the details panel, and controls the enabled/disabled state of the "Apply from this date" button based on the day’s status.

First-open-day form interaction: Typing a date string into an input and submitting triggers validation (format 'YYYY-MM-DD'), updates the URL fromDate parameter, calls getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate), and then scrolls/updates the calendar to display and highlight the returned open day if found.

Apply button interaction: Clicking "Apply from this date" triggers startApplicationFromCalendarDate with the selected date; on success it reads application.id from the response and navigates to application.html?applicationId=<id>.

URL-driven initialization: On page load or when the URL changes (e.g., via browser navigation), the page reads year, month, and fromDate, determines which interfaces to call (getCalendarMonthDays and optionally getFirstOpenDayAfter), and reconstructs the calendar view and selected day from those parameters and API responses.

Error and edge-case interaction: If the user inputs an invalid date format into the first-open-day form, the form displays a validation message and does not call getFirstOpenDayAfter until the format is corrected; if getCalendarMonthDays returns no data for a month, the calendar grid shows an appropriate empty-state message.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page’s functional state is driven by URL parameters and interface responses. On load, the page reads year, month, and fromDate from the query string. If year and month are present and valid, they are used for the calendar; otherwise, the page derives year and month from the current system date (this is dynamic, not hard-coded) and updates the URL to reflect them. The page always calls getCalendarMonthDays(year, month) whenever year or month changes. If fromDate is present and valid, the page also calls getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate). If getFirstOpenDayAfter returns a non-empty date, the page derives the corresponding year and month from that date, updates the URL to those values (ensuring consistency between the highlighted date and the visible month), and reloads the month via getCalendarMonthDays if the month/year changed. It then highlights the returned calendarDay as the selected day in UI state; this selection is not stored in the URL but is fully reproducible by re-running getFirstOpenDayAfter with the same fromDate. If getFirstOpenDayAfter returns an empty date, the page leaves year/month as-is and simply indicates that no open day is available after fromDate. When the user navigates months using UI controls, the page updates year and month in the URL and re-calls getCalendarMonthDays; if a fromDate is in the URL, it is left unchanged unless the user explicitly changes it via the first-open-day form. The startApplicationFromCalendarDate interface does not change the URL parameters on calendar.html; instead, after receiving the Application object it triggers navigation to application.html with applicationId taken from the response. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; all restorable state flows from URL parameters and fresh interface calls.

components.functionality: Reads URL parameters (year, month, fromDate) on page load or URL change, determines the initial calendar month to display, triggers calls to getCalendarMonthDays(year, month), and optionally getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate). Aggregates the results and initializes shared page state (currentYear, currentMonth, calendarDays, selectedDay, firstOpenDayResult).

Displays the current month and year and provides controls to navigate to the previous or next month, and optionally a selector to jump to a specific month and year. When the user changes the month/year, it updates the URL and triggers a refresh of the calendar grid.

Renders a monthly grid of dates for the current year and month. Each cell is mapped to a CalendarDay item returned by getCalendarMonthDays(year, month), indicating status (open, closed, partial) and note. Handles click events to select a day, and visually distinguishes the selected day and, if applicable, the first-open-day result.

Shows details of the currently selected day from the calendar grid, including the date, status, and note from the CalendarDay model. Determines whether the date is eligible for starting an application (for example, status 'open' or possibly 'partial' according to business rules) and enables or disables the "Apply from this date" button accordingly. When the button is clicked, it calls startApplicationFromCalendarDate(desiredStartDate) and, on success, navigates to application.html with the returned applicationId.

Provides an input field for a reference date (format 'YYYY-MM-DD') and a submit action to find the first open day after that date. On submit, validates the format, updates the fromDate URL parameter, calls getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate), and updates page state so that the month/year and calendar grid align with the returned open day. If no open day is found, it updates the detail panel with a message and leaves the calendar month selection unchanged.

Central component that reads currentYear, currentMonth, and currentFromDate from page state and keeps the browser URL query parameters (year, month, fromDate) in sync whenever these values change due to user actions (month navigation, first-open-day search, initial load). Also listens for browser back/forward navigation and triggers re-initialization via calendar-page-initializer.

Displays user feedback when getFirstOpenDayAfter(fromDate) returns an object whose date field is empty, indicating that no open days are available after the requested date. This component is driven solely by the result of getFirstOpenDayAfter and does not make interface calls on its own.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a stacked top-to-bottom structure. At the top of the main content area, a rounded, card-like 'month hero' contains the Calendar & Closures title, the active month label, and month navigation arrows, echoing the hero slider from the mockup. Directly beneath this, inside the same centered container, a two-column layout appears: the left column holds the calendar grid (the main interactive area), and the right column vertically stacks the day detail panel, the first-open-day finder form, and any feedback message. Invisible technical components (initializer and URL state sync) sit in the background without occupying space. A small scroll-to-top button (as in the mockup) floats at the bottom-right of the viewport outside the main flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a stacked top-to-bottom structure. At the top of the main content area, a rounded, card-like 'month hero' contains the Calendar & Closures title, the active month label, and month navigation arrows, echoing the hero slider from the mockup. Directly beneath this, inside the same centered container, a two-column layout appears: the left column holds the calendar grid (the main interactive area), and the right column vertically stacks the day detail panel, the first-open-day finder form, and any feedback message. Invisible technical components (initializer and URL state sync) sit in the background without occupying space. A small scroll-to-top button (as in the mockup) floats at the bottom-right of the viewport outside the main flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The main content lives inside a centered, fixed-width container (about 1100–1200px wide) on a softly colored full-width background. The top month header/hero spans the full width of this container inside a rounded rectangle with generous internal padding (~24–32px). Below it, the two-column section allocates roughly 55–60% of the width to the left calendar grid and 40–45% to the right column with details and tools. Vertical spacing between the top month hero and the two-column section, and between major blocks within each column, follows the 48–64px section spacing pattern, with inner separations around 24–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density is moderate-to-low: large, friendly type for month titles and day numbers, generous line height for labels and explanations, and plenty of whitespace between calendar weeks, panels in the right column, and interactive controls. Each main block (month header, calendar grid, detail panel, first-open-day finder, feedback message) appears as a comfortable card with 24–32px internal padding, rounded corners, and breathing room between elements (16–24px between headings, form fields, and paragraphs). The layout feels uncluttered and approachable for busy parents.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the top-left with the page and month title in the month header, then moves to the top-right where the month navigation arrows sit, similar to the mockup's hero slider controls. From there, attention naturally drops to the large calendar grid on the left, scanning across rows of days. When a day is selected or when searching for the first open day, the user’s focus is gently pulled to the right column, where the day detail panel and finder form live as secondary focal areas. The overall motion resembles a Z-pattern across the hero, then an F-shaped scan down the calendar grid, with the colorful, card-like panels on the right acting like the illustration counterpart—visually supportive and balancing the composition. The floating scroll-to-top button anchors the bottom-right corner as a subtle persistent element.

layout.overall_description: The Calendar & Closures page sits on a warm, softly tinted background with watercolor-style accents at the edges, while the main content is centered in a fixed-width container. At the very top of this container, a large rounded rectangle acts as the month hero: on the left, a chunky, friendly page title like 'Naptár & Zárvatartás' sits above the current month and year; on the right, within the same band, playful arrow buttons allow users to jump to previous or next months, echoing the hero slider’s navigation in the mockup. There is ample padding inside this hero so the text and controls feel airy. Below this hero, separated by a comfortable vertical gap, the page splits into two columns. The left column is wider and holds the calendar grid in a rounded card that nearly fills that column, with each day square clearly separated and non-working or closed days indicated by soft, watercolor-like highlight fills. The right column is slightly narrower and vertically stacks two to three cards: first a day detail panel showing selected-day information, then the first-open-day finder form, and, when needed, a feedback panel if no open days are found. Each of these right-column cards has its own rounded box, soft background, and generous padding. Invisible logic components do not interrupt this visual rhythm. As the user scrolls, all these cards move together in a smooth, linear flow, while a small, rounded scroll-to-top button floats near the bottom-right of the viewport, subtly echoing the mockup’s fixed utility control and maintaining a sense of friendly, guided navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component operates behind the scenes and does not render any visible UI. It is mounted near the top of the page’s main content structure—logically just before the month hero—but it does not occupy any visible space or alter the flow of other elements. The month header appears immediately below the global site header, as if the initializer were not present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral component with no layout footprint; it sits in the DOM but participates only in initialization logic and does not affect spacing or positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The month header sits at the top of the main content area inside a large, horizontally oriented rounded rectangle that spans roughly 80–90% of the viewport width within the centered container. It is positioned directly beneath the global site header, separated by a vertical gap of about 48–64px to give it breathing room. Internally, it uses a two-part horizontal layout: on the left two-thirds, the page title ('Naptár & Zárvatartás') appears in a large, playful display font, with the current month and year directly below in a slightly smaller but still prominent style. On the right third, aligned to the vertical center, sit the month navigation controls: two rounded arrow buttons (previous/next month) and possibly a compact month dropdown, visually grouped like the slider arrows in the mockup. Padding around the content is generous (about 24–32px on all sides), and the header’s background is a warm, light tone contrasting softly with the outer page background. This header visually anchors the rest of the page, with the calendar grid beginning right below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element in normal document flow. It scrolls out of view when the user moves down the page and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The calendar grid is the main interactive element and occupies the left side of a two-column section directly beneath the month header. This section begins about 32–48px below the bottom of the month header. The grid’s container is a large rounded card that fills approximately 55–60% of the horizontal space of the centered content area, aligned to the left. The right column begins about 24–32px to the right of the grid card. Inside the grid card, there is 24px padding on all sides. At the top of the card, weekday labels form a horizontal row aligned left to right, with consistent spacing. Below them, the days of the month appear in a 7-column grid. Each day cell is square or slightly taller than wide, with at least 8–12px internal padding around the day number, and 8px gaps between cells to prevent a cramped look. Closed days use soft, watercolor-style background fills and subtle icons (like a small lock or moon), while open days remain lighter with a clear numeric emphasis. Week rows are separated by 12–16px vertical gaps to keep density low. When a day is selected, its cell receives a rounded highlight (slightly thicker border or filled pill) that is clearly visible against the playful palette. The entire grid card stretches vertically as needed for 4–6 week rows but leaves a comfortable margin (32–48px) at the bottom before the next section content begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main two-column layout in normal document flow. It adjusts its width responsively within the fixed container but remains aligned to the left column and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The day detail panel sits in the right column, aligned with the top of the calendar grid so that their top edges are horizontally level. It occupies the full width of the right column, which is approximately 40–45% of the content width. The panel itself is a vertically oriented rounded card with a soft background color that visually echoes the illustration area from the mockup: it feels like a cozy information card complementing the more structured calendar on the left. Inside, a clear heading—such as the selected date and its status ('Nyitva' / 'Zárva')—appears at the top with 16–24px margin below, followed by detail text like opening hours, notes, and a prominent button or link to 'Start Application' when the day is open. Internal padding is generous (24–32px), with content separated by 12–16px vertical gaps to keep a light editorial feel. When no day is selected, the panel can show a friendly placeholder illustration or short message centered in the card, preserving the visual balance with the left calendar. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of this panel and the top of the first-open-day finder form below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card within the right column, scrolling with the page in normal document flow. It does not overlay or float; instead it maintains a stable position relative to the calendar grid beside it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The first-open-day finder is placed directly below the day detail panel in the right column, separated by a 24–32px vertical gap. It also spans the full width of the right column and appears as a slightly smaller rounded card to signal its secondary, tool-like role. Inside the card, a short, bold prompt at the top explains the function (e.g., 'Keresse meg az első nyitott napot…'), followed by one or two form controls—such as a date picker or starting date field and a dropdown for nursery selection—arranged in a vertical stack with 12–16px spacing between fields. At the bottom-right of the card, a prominent action button ('Keresés') sits with enough padding around it (8–12px inside the button, and 16–24px space above) to maintain the playful, open feeling. The card uses 24px internal padding around all edges and is aligned so its left edge lines up with the left edge of the day detail panel above it, preserving a clean vertical rhythm. Below this form, when applicable, the resulting suggested date is highlighted in a short line or chip-like element, which aligns left within the card and visually ties back to the calendar grid on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block element within the right column, stacked under the day detail panel. It scrolls with the page and does not have any fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is entirely non-visual. It lives in the background at the page level, conceptually associated with the calendar grid and month header, but it does not render any UI or consume visible space. Whether it is placed near the top or bottom of the component tree, the user experiences a continuous layout: month header, then two-column content, with no sign of this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual behavioral component; it has no spatial representation and does not alter layout, margins, or the stacking of other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-open-day message appears conditionally within the right column, directly beneath the first-open-day finder card. When displayed, it is separated from the finder by a 16–24px vertical gap. The message is contained in a compact, rounded feedback card that spans the full width of the right column but is visually lighter than the primary cards—using perhaps a softer border and a pale yellow or pastel background reminiscent of the numbered circles from the mockup’s welcome section. Inside, a short, empathetic heading (e.g., 'Nincs elérhető nyitott nap ebben az időszakban') appears at the top, with one or two lines of explanatory text below, each separated by about 8–12px. The internal padding is around 16–24px on all sides, keeping the card airy yet clearly noticeable. This card does not push into the left column; instead, it maintains the same left and right alignment as the other right-column cards, preserving the overall two-column structure. When hidden, the space collapses so the right column content above shifts naturally toward the bottom of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional block element in normal document flow. It appears and disappears based on state, expanding or collapsing vertical space beneath the first-open-day finder, and scrolls naturally with the rest of the page.

name: Contact / General Inquiry
title: Contact / General Inquiry
description: This page lets parents send a general inquiry to the municipal nursery service, choose a subject/category, optionally link the message to a specific nursery, and receive a confirmation once the inquiry is submitted.

core_features: Load the available inquiry subjects/categories dynamically using the getContactSubjects interface and populate the subject dropdown when the page is opened.
Display a contact form that collects the user’s name, email address, optional phone number, selected subject, and free-text message.
Preselect the subject in the dropdown based on the optional subject URL parameter when it matches one of the subjects returned by getContactSubjects.
Associate the inquiry with a specific nursery by reading the optional nurseryId URL parameter and including it as a hidden or non-editable field in the form submission to submitContactInquiry.
Validate required form fields on submit (at least name, email, subject, and message) and prevent submission until they are present and correctly formatted.
Submit the completed form via the submitContactInquiry interface, handle loading/disabled states during submission, and display success or error feedback based on the interface response.
After a successful submission, display a confirmation panel summarizing that the inquiry was received, using data from the submitContactInquiry response.
Provide a "Back to home" navigation action on the confirmation panel that routes the user to index.html without additional parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Generic contact from header or footer:
1) User clicks "Contact us" in the header or "Contact" in the footer, opening contact.html without URL parameters.
2) On page load, the page-state-manager triggers getContactSubjects to retrieve the subject options.
3) The subject dropdown is populated once the options load; a loading indicator is shown until then and an error message is shown if loading fails.
4) The user fills in their name, email, optionally a phone number, selects a subject from the dropdown, and writes their message.
5) The user clicks the form submit button. Client-side validations run; invalid or missing required fields are highlighted and submission is blocked until fixed.
6) If validation passes, contact-form calls submitContactInquiry with the current form values (including nurseryId set to an empty/undefined value if not provided by URL).
7) While awaiting a response, the submit button is disabled and a submitting state is shown.
8) On successful response, the page switches to a confirmation state and shows the submission-result-panel. If the interface returns an error, the error is shown and the form remains visible for corrections.

Workflow 2 – Inquiry starting from a specific nursery detail page:
1) On nursery_detail.html, the user clicks a link or button to contact the service (for example from meals, applications, or visits sections). The site navigates to contact.html with URL parameters subject and nurseryId set appropriately.
2) On load, page-state-manager reads the subject and nurseryId URL parameters and stores them in page state.
3) getContactSubjects is called. When subject options are returned, contact-subject-loader attempts to match the subject from the URL parameter against the available options.
4) If a match is found, the subject dropdown is preselected with that option; if there is no match, no option is preselected and the user must choose one manually.
5) The form includes an indication that the inquiry will be linked to the referenced nursery via nurseryId. The nurseryId is stored and included unmodified in the submitContactInquiry call.
6) The user completes the remaining fields (name, email, phone, message) and submits the form.
7) submitContactInquiry is called with subject, message, parentName, email, phone, and nurseryId from state.
8) On success, the confirmation panel notes that the inquiry has been sent and is associated (internally) with the provided nurseryId.

Workflow 3 – Handling validation or submission errors and resubmission:
1) The user attempts to submit the form with missing or invalid data (e.g., missing email or message). Client-side validation detects issues and shows field-level messages, and no API call is made until all required fields are valid.
2) If the API call to submitContactInquiry fails (e.g., network/server issue), the submission-result-panel (or an inline form error area) displays an error message generated from the failure state.
3) The form remains editable; the user can adjust any fields if appropriate and click submit again.
4) On successful resubmission, the previous error message is cleared, the form is locked into a submitted state, and the confirmation panel is displayed.
5) From the confirmation panel, the user can choose to navigate back to the home page via the "Back to home" action, which routes to index.html.
interactions: On page load, page-state-manager reads the subject and nurseryId URL parameters (if present) and initializes internal state accordingly (including a pending subject preselection value and nursery association).

On page load (after state initialization), contact-subject-loader invokes getContactSubjects; while waiting, it sets a loading state that disables the subject dropdown and may display a loading indicator.

When getContactSubjects succeeds, contact-subject-loader populates the subject dropdown options and checks whether the URL-derived subject matches one of these options; if so, it sets the current subject selection to that value.

If getContactSubjects fails, contact-subject-loader sets an error state that displays an error message near the subject field and prevents form submission until the subjects are successfully reloaded or the page is refreshed.
When the user changes the subject dropdown selection (onChange), contact-form updates the subject field in internal form state and clears any subject-specific validation errors.

When the user types into the name, email, phone, or message fields (onChange / onInput), contact-form updates the corresponding fields in form state and may clear field-level validation errors for those fields.

When the user submits the form (onSubmit event on the form), contact-form performs client-side validation for required fields and email format; if validation fails, it prevents calling submitContactInquiry and highlights the invalid fields.

If client-side validation passes on submit, contact-form calls submitContactInquiry with the current form state values (subject, message, parentName, email, phone, nurseryId) and switches the form into a submitting state (e.g., disables inputs and submit button).

When submitContactInquiry returns a success response, page-state-manager stores the returned ContactInquiry object and success message, switches the page into a submitted/success state, hides or disables the form, and shows the submission-result-panel.

If submitContactInquiry returns an error or the call fails, page-state-manager sets an error state and displays a submission error message in the form or in a dedicated error area; the form is re-enabled so the user can correct and resubmit.
On clicking the "Back to home" action from the submission-result-panel (onClick), back-to-home-link navigates the browser to index.html without additional URL parameters.

If the user navigates back to contact.html using the browser’s back button after a successful submission, page-state-manager re-reads the URL parameters and reconstructs the initial state; whether the form or the confirmation panel appears depends on whether the implementation chooses to reset the submitted state or persist it in memory for that session.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is primarily driven by URL parameters and interface responses. On initial load, the page reads the subject and nurseryId parameters from the query string of contact.html (for example, contact.html?subject=...&nurseryId=...). These values are stored in internal state as initialSubjectParam and initialNurseryIdParam. The initialSubjectParam is not immediately applied to the subject dropdown; instead, after getContactSubjects returns its dynamic list, the code checks whether any returned subject option value matches initialSubjectParam. If a match is found, the subject dropdown’s selected value is set to that subject; if no match is found, no preselection occurs and the user must pick a subject manually. The initialNurseryIdParam is stored and displayed as read-only contextual information, and it is passed unchanged as nurseryId into submitContactInquiry whenever the form is submitted. If nurseryId is not present in the URL, the form state holds a null/undefined nurseryId, and submitContactInquiry is called without nursery association. The page has an internal status flag that can be in one of several states, such as 'initial-loading-subjects', 'ready', 'submitting', 'submit-success', or 'submit-error'. Transitions occur on specific events (subject load success/failure, form submit, API response). URL parameters are not modified by the page after load; navigation to index.html occurs via a simple link or redirect to index.html. Any revisits to contact.html rely on whatever subject and nurseryId parameters are present in the new URL.

components.functionality: Initializes and manages the overall state of the Contact / General Inquiry page, including reading URL parameters, tracking loading and submission states, and coordinating data between the subject loader, contact form, and submission result panel.

Fetches the list of available contact subjects/categories using getContactSubjects on page load, exposes the options to the contact form, and applies any preselection based on the subject URL parameter when there is a matching value.

Reflects whether the current inquiry is associated with a specific nursery by reading the nurseryId from page-state-manager. If nurseryId is present, it shows a contextual note or non-editable field and ensures that nurseryId is included in the payload when the form is submitted.

Provides input fields for parentName, email, optional phone, subject selection (using the dynamically loaded subject options), and message. Performs client-side validation on submit, and when valid, calls submitContactInquiry with the current form data and nurseryId context.

Displays the outcome after a submission attempt. On success, shows a confirmation message and may access fields from the returned ContactInquiry (such as subject or createdAt if exposed) to confirm that the message was received. On error, shows an error message and provides a path back to the editable form.
Offers a simple navigation control from the confirmation state to return the user to the home page at index.html after a successful contact inquiry submission.

layout.grid_system: Appears to follow a centered, fixed-width container on a fluid background, likely based on a 12‑column grid. The welcome section uses a two‑column grid (approx. 7 columns text, 5 columns illustration).
layout.layout_pattern: Use a centered, fixed-width main container on a soft, full-width background. Inside, a single large rounded rectangle acts as the main contact card, visually echoing the hero slider from the mockup. The card is divided into two stacked zones: a compact top header band (contact title + brief intro + nursery context indicator) and, directly below, a two-column content area where the contact form occupies the left column and supportive/contextual information (including the nursery context indicator on some breakpoints) sits on the right. Below the main card, a small back-to-home link sits aligned to the left. The submission-result panel reuses the same card footprint, replacing the form when active. Non-visual manager/loader components sit behind, controlling which of these states is shown.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a centered, fixed-width main container on a soft, full-width background.
